The settlement demand that follows, as well as the medical records and bills, is presented to resolve our client’s claim. We understand your company relies on computer software programs to evaluate personal injury claims. This settlement is designed and organized specifically for the purpose of making the evaluation as effective as possible.

All the value drivers and factors presented in the demand were taken directly from the medical records and bills. The injuries and damages are submitted based on our knowledge and understanding of our client's history and expected future losses. We reserve the right to amend this demand at any time in the future to accurately reflect our client's situation.

L&D Delivery Note L&D Delivery Note by Terrassa, Margarita, MD at 1/30/2021 1926

BH Louisville Cesarean Section Operative Note

Pre-Operative Dx: 1. IUP at 41w1d weeks 2. Failure to Progress

Postoperative Dx: Same

Procedure: C-Section, Low Transverse Surgeon: Margarita Terrassa Assistant: Dr Groves

Anesthesia: Epidural

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 41




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1676], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-1675] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) L&D Delivery Note (continued)

Findings: Amniotic Fluid clear Uterus normal Tubes and ovaries normal bilaterally

Infant: Gender: Viable male infant Weight: 3695 g (8 Ib 2.3 oz) Apgars: 4 @1 minute /

8 @ 5 minutes

Indication for C/Section: Failure to Progress

Procedure Details: The patient was taken to the operating room where epidural anesthesia was found to be adequate. She was prepped and draped in the usual sterile manner in the dorsal supine position with leftward tilt. A Pfannenstiel incision was made and carried down to the fascia. The fascia was incised in the mid-line and extended transversely with Mayo scissors. The fascia was grasped and elevated and separated from the underlying rectus muscles superiorly and inferiorly. The peritoneum was identified and entered. Peritoneal incision was extended superiorly and inferiorly with good visualization of the bladder. The bladder blade was inserted and the vesicouterine peritoneum was incised transversely and the bladder flap was created digitally. The bladder blade was reinserted. The lower uterine segment was incised in a transverse fashion. The head was elevated and delivered through the incision. The remainder of the infant was delivered without difficulty. The umbilical cord was clamped and cut and the infant was handed off the field. Cord ph and cord bloods were obtained. The placenta was removed intact and appeared normal. The uterine incision was closed with running locked sutures of 0 monocryl. The abdominal cavity was irrigated and cleared of all clot and debris. The uterine incision was inspected and noted to be hemostatic. Rectus muscles were reapproximated in the midline with O chromic. The fascia was closed with 0 PDS in running continuous fashion. The subcutaneous tissue was closed with 3-0 chromic. The skin was closed in subcuticular fashion with 4-0 Monocryl. Instrument, sponge, and needle counts were correct prior the abdominal closure and at the conclusion of the case. Mother and baby to recovery room in stable condition.

Complications: none

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 42

This note was copied from a baby's chart.

Mother has some blistering on both nipples, reports some pinching with latch. Infant currently latched to left nipple, upon release nipple lipstick shaped. Discussed the importance of asymmetrical latch and how to achieve deep latch. Assisted mother with football positioning on right side, demonstrated deep asymmetrical latch. Mother reports no pain or pinching with latch. Discussed how to determine nutritive sucks and audible swallows noted. Encouraged mother to provide support and guidance to nipple for infant to achieve proper latch. Discussed when to expect milk

to come in and ways to tell infant is getting enough. Encouraged mother to call for assistance or questions as needed.

Dr Lewis to bedside after RN request for MD to review strip. MD discussed with patient the need for IUPC placement and amnioinfusion to resolve variable decelerations. McClish, Sarah, RN at 1/30/2021 1945

Author: McClish, Sarah, RN Service: Obstetrics Author Type: Registered Nurse Filed: 01/31/21 0749 Date of Service: 01/30/21 1945 Creation Time: 01/31/21 0727 Status: Signed Editor: McClish, Sarah, RN (Registered Nurse)

Summary: Father's behavior in OR

Late entry:

OR case started at 1848. FOB was told that we will observe moment of silence as much as possible upon delivery of baby. FOB verbalized understanding that if there were any complications with baby, there would not be absolute silence. Birth time was at 1855. Baby was still on abdomen and FOB tried to walk around to the sterile field after being asked by AA (Dr Lammers) to stay seated. | asked the FOB to sit down as well and he pushed his body toward me to get me to move. | put my hands in front of my body to keep him away from me.

Baby went directly to warmer for resuscitative measures (See notes on delivery record) where three nurses including NNP were assisting in care. FOB was getting loud and trying to push his way toward baby in order to whisper his prayer in baby's ear. | told him that baby needed assistance breathing at the moment and that he can sit rignt where he was (less than 1 foot away from baby) and say his prayer. FOB tried to push his way to warmer which is when | put my hands in front of my body again and told him he will have to leave the OR if he does not sit down. Dr Lammers stood up and at the same time Dr Pennell came into OR. FOB sat down.

FOB stayed seated after this but continued to interrupt resuscitative efforts by making rude comments toward staff and doctors. Breathing efforts toward baby continued until 10 minutes. FOB said more than one prayer during this time with no attempt at interruptions of prayer by any staff.

Electronically signed by McClish, Sarah, RN at 01/31/21 0749

Hurst, Megan Rachelle Parker, RN at 1/31/2021 0535

Anderson, Claire Elise, RN at 1/31/2021 0731

Author: Anderson, Claire Elise, RN Service: Obstetrics Author Type: Registered Nurse Filed: 01/31/21 0929 Date of Service: 01/31/21 0731 Creation Time: 01/31/21 0731 Status: Signed Editor: Anderson, Claire Elise, RN (Registered Nurse)

Late entry

Summary of day

Patient called out requesting information about epidural at 1100. This RN explained the epidural process. Patient states she would like an epidural at this time. Patient's significant other states " Do not give it to her unless | tell you. She doesn't really want it." This RN explained to the patient's significant other that it is the patient's choice and that | will get anesthesia if the patient asks for an epidural. He proceeded to tell me "leave the room because it's not your decision." The patient states that she was okay to discuss with her significant other.

Dr. Sherman was at bedside at 1128 for epidural placement. The patient's significant other was told to take a seat during the procedure and he responded "| wont pass out, I've killed people before so I'm not sitting down."

At 1634, Dr. Terrassa was at bedside to discuss plan of care with patient and significant other. MD discussed risks and benefits of cesarean delivery. The patient's significant other stated " We will not be doing a c-section, | believe in God over science."

At 1645 the patient's significant other came out to the nurses station to talk with this RN in the hallway. The patient's significant other told this RN that they would refuse the cesarean section if we could not promise a minute of silence at delivery. Discussed with significant other normal protocols for our staff in a c-section and why it is important for us to be able to vocalize amniotic fluid color, baby birth time, bleeding, etc but that we would try to limit these to accommodate for a moment of silence for prayer. This RN told the significant other that we could not promise that because each delivery is different and we can not predict how the baby will come out at delivery and if he would need special attention due to patient risk factors. The significant other then proceeded to say that "we will risk the baby and mom's life if | can not say a prayer to my son and the room cannot be silent. My son's first words that he hears will not be coming from you all." This RN told him we would do everything we can to make that possible but can not promise.

At 1655 The patient's significant other stated to this RN " If we do the c-section | do not think the patient needed any other numbing medicine." This RN explained that the c-section is abdominal surgery and we would have to give her stronger medicine so she was comfortable during the procedure. He proceeded to say " My family has addiction problems and you will not make my baby addicted to any medications." This RN told him that is part of the c-section and that | can get anesthesia to the room to talk about medications if they decide to proceed with the c-section.

At 1659 the patient called out and said she would like to proceed with a cesarean delivery at this time. This RN again explained that we would try to have a minute of silence at delivery but could not promise at this time. Also explained no video in the OR was allowed and Significant other would have to be seated during the entire procedure behind the sterile drape. He would not be allowed to stand or go into the sterile field. Both patient and significant other agreed to this.

Electronically signed by Anderson, Claire Elise, RN at 01/31/21 0929

Progress Notes Lewis, Lauren S, MD at 1/30/2021 0609

Author: Lewis, Lauren S, MD Service: Gynecology Author Type: Physician Filed: 01/30/21 0611 Date of Service: 01/30/21 0609 Creation Time: 01/30/21 0609 Status: Signed Editor: Lewis, Lauren S, MD (Physician)

Tolerating labor well, sleeping between contractions. IUPC placed around 3AM due to recurrent variable decels, now improved s/p amnioinfusion. At 3AM cervix was 3/90/0. UCs not yet adequate so working to titrate pitocin. EFW last week per u/s was 8lb5oz so did discuss potential for cephalopelvic disproportion. Will continue to titrate pitocin and once adequate if no further progress this AM discussed need to consider cesarean delivery

Lauren Lewis, MD

Electronically signed by Lewis, Lauren S, MD at 01/30/21 0611

Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued) Terrassa, Margarita, MD at 1/30/2021 1633

Author: Terrassa, Margarita, MD Service: Gynecology Author Type: Physician Filed: 01/30/21 1640 Date of Service: 01/30/21 1633 Creation Time: 01/30/21 1633 Status: Signed Editor: Terrassa, Margarita, MD (Physician)

Reviewed in detail noted slow progress of labor increased risk of PP bleeding due to over saturation of uterine receptor for prolonged labor and increased risk of infection due to ROM >24. Also, noted moderate caput and suspect Cephalopelvic Disproportion and rec to proceed with CD to avoid bleeding complications in light of clinically labor not progressing and now seeing decrease variability in FHT's. Pt given time to ask questions and will like to discuss with FOB who mentioned that "he does not believe in science and he will like to let God decide". Also, that having a CD will not allow him to do what he will like to do/ Reviewed in detail that | even if she is having minimal cervical change | suspect that patient will have failure to descend in light of pelvic exam and progression of labor/

Electronically signed by Terrassa, Margarita, MD at 01/30/21 1640

[DOB-1628] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued) Patient Name: Anella Noble MRN: [PHONE-168] Today's Date: 2/2/2021 Admit Date: 1/28/2021 Discharge Needs Assessment No documentation.

Discharge Plan

Row Name 02/02/21 1037

Plan

Plan Infant to discharge home with mother when medically ready. Deena Green, CSW

Plan Comments Mother: Anella Noble, MRN [PHONE-1626]; Infant: AnellasBoy “Alik” Noble, MRN

[PHONE-1625]. CSW was consulted for “Social support,” and “hx of abuse per report, family dynamics concerns.” Of note, no urine toxicology obtained on mother nor infant. Cord toxicology was not sent. No indication of need for toxicology screens at this time. CSW spoke with RN Mary Jude M, who advised that father is controlling and loud, but not violent or threatening. CSW met with mother alone at bedside. Mother verified address, phone, and insurance. Mother reports she has spoken with MedAssist about adding infant to insurance coverage. Mother reports that she and father are in the process of moving, but mother did not disclose new address. Mother reports they have car seat, crib, clothes, diapers and other supplies. Mother is not current with WIC, is breast feeding. CSW educated mother about WIC program. Mother plans on follow up with Dr. Kahttah and is comfortable scheduling appointments and will have transportation to appointments. Mother reports that maternal grandmother is involved and supportive. Mother denies any violence, threats, or feeling unsafe. HANDS program discussed with mother, mother agreeable to HANDS referral. CSW made HANDS referral via secure email. Mother was cooperative and polite during discussion. Mother with bright affect and good eye contact during discussion. Mother denied any other needs or concerns. CSW provided mother with packet of resources info and briefly discussed resources in packet. Packet includes info on: HANDS, WIC, domestic violence resources, infant supply resources, transportation, counseling, and general community resources. Deena Green, CSW

Continued Care and Services - Admitted Since 1/28/2021 Coordination has not been started for this encounter.

1. Identification of Patient, key family member(s) or patient surrogate

2. Identification of the responsible Practitioner (primary service)

[DOB-1445] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Other Orders (group 2 of 6) (continued)

Acknowledged: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0054 for Placing Order Culver, Amy Christine, RN 01/30/21 2309 for D/C Order Admin instructions: Give IV push if:

Maternal systolic blood pressure drops below 100 mmHg or 10% drop from baseline

Fetal heart rate deceleration (20 beats per minute below baseline lasting more than 30 seconds) occurs with in 20 minutes of epidural dosing Discuss dosing with Anesthesia in patients with pre-eclampsia, hypertension, cardiac history or tocolytic

Caution: Look alike/sound alike drug alert Dilute with NS to 5-10 mg/mL. Central line preferred, if unavailable use large bore IV access with frequent nurse monitoring of IV site.

oxytocin in sodium chloride (PITOCIN) 30 UNIT/500ML infusion solution [342791827] (Discontinued)

Electronically signed by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD on 01/30/21 1308 Status: Discontinued Mode: Ordering in Telephone with readback mode Communicated by: Sylvia, Kayla, RN

Ordering user: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0223 Ordering provider: Terrassa, Margarita, MD

Authorized by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD Ordering mode: Telephone with readback

Frequency: Routine Titrated 01/29/21 0315 - 2 days Class: Normal

Discontinued by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD 01/30/21 2137

Acknowledged: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0443 for Placing Order Culver, Amy Christine, RN 01/30/21 2309 for D/C Order Admin instructions: Start at 2 milli-units/min and titrate up or down by 2 milli-units every 30 min to a max of 20 milli-units/min. Titrate to maternal/fetal

tolerance, with a goal of achieving an adequate labor pattern defined by

progressive cervical effacement and cervical dilation of approximately 0.5

cm/hr to 1 cm/hr once active labor is achieved. Contractions should not be

more frequent than every 2 minutes. Notify physician for tachysystole.

Status: Discontinued Communicated by: McClellan, Lena R, RN Ordering provider: Lewis, Lauren S, MD Ordering mode: Telephone with readback Class: Hospital Performed Instance released by: McClellan, Lena R, RN (auto-released) 1/28/2021 8:32 PM

Order comments: NO TREATMENT RECOMMENDED IF: 1) Maternal GBS Status Known Negative 2) Scheduled Cesarean Birth With Intact Membranes, Not in Labor 3) Maternal GBS Status Unknown, No Risk Factors TREAT WITH ANTIBIOTICS IF: 1) Maternal GBS Status Known Positive 2) Maternal GBS Status Unknown With Risk Factors: a) Previous Infant Affected By GBS Infection b) GBS Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Bacteriuria During Pregnancy c) Unexplained Maternal Fever (100.4F (38C) or Greater) During Labor d) Prolonged Rupture of Membranes (18 or More Hours) e) Gestational Age Less Than 37 Weeks

1/30/2021 5:06 PM Discontinued by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD 01/30/21 2137 Order comments: Indwelling Urinary Catheter Removal Criteria Discontinue Indwelling Urinary Catheter Unless One of the Following is Present: Urinary Retention or Obstruction Chronic Urinary Catheter Use End of Life Critical Illness with Strict I/O GU Tract or Abdominal Surgery Stage 3/4 Sacral / Perineal Wound Required Activity Restriction: Trauma Required Activity Restriction: Spine Surgery If Patient is Being Followed by Urology Contact Them PRIOR to Removal Do Not Remove Indwelling Urinary Catheter Order is Present with a CLINICAL REASON to Maintain the Catheter. Provider is Required to Include a Clinical Reason to Maintain a Urinary Catheter Patient Admitted With Indwelling Urinary Catheter (Not Placed at Baptist Facility) Assess for Continued Need & Document Medical Necessity If Infection is Suspected, Contact the Provider

Discharge Instructions [343607219] (Active)

Electronically signed by: Grider, Ann R., MD on 02/03/21 1120 Status: Active Ordering user: Grider, Ann R., MD 02/03/21 1120 Ordering provider: Grider, Ann R., MD

Authorized by: Grider, Ann R., MD Ordering mode: Standard

Frequency: Routine 02/03/21 - Class: Clinic Performed

Quantity: 1

Order comments: Nothing vaginally until after seen in office. Bath, shower, stairs ok. Driving is ok when the baby is a week old and if you are not taking percocet/narcotics. No intentional exercise or heavy lifting over 10 Ibs. If something hurts, don't do it. Call for heavy vaginal bleeding more than soaking a pad/hour, fever of 101. Follow up with Women First in 6 weeks. The office should contact you to make appointment within a week of discharge.

ready. Deena Green, CSW -DG at 02/02/21 1037 Plan Comments Mother: Anella Noble, MRN [PHONE-1350]; Infant: AnellasBoy “Alik” Noble, MRN [PHONE-446]. CSW was consulted for “Social support,” and “hx of abuse per report, family dynamics concerns.” Of note, no urine toxicology obtained on mother nor infant. Cord toxicology was not sent. No indication of need for toxicology screens at this time. CSW spoke with RN Mary Jude M, who advised that father is controlling and loud, but not violent or threatening. CSW met with mother alone at bedside. Mother verified address, phone, and insurance. Mother reports she has spoken with MedAssist about adding infant to insurance coverage. Mother reports that she and father are in the process of moving, but mother did not disclose new address. Mother reports they have car seat, crib, clothes, diapers and other supplies. Mother is not current with WIC, is breast feeding. CSW educated mother about WIC program. Mother plans on follow up with Dr. Kahttah and is comfortable scheduling appointments and will have transportation to appointments. Mother reports that maternal

grandmother is involved and supportive. Mother denies any violence, threats, or feeling unsafe. HANDS program discussed with mother, mother agreeable to HANDS referral. Mother denied any other needs or concerns. CSW provided mother with packet of resources info and briefly discussed resources in packet. Care Plan

Encounter Problems Encounter Problems (Resolved) Problem: Adjustment to Role Transition (Postpartum Cesarean Delivery)

Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Successful Maternal Role Transition (Resolved) Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Support Maternal Role Transition (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Develop trust, relationship and rapport.

Use a family-focused approach; promote involvement of support system in infant care. Incorporate cultural beliefs, rituals and practices in family-centered care.

Encourage and support breastfeeding, frequent holding and skin-to-skin contact with newborn. Encourage attachment behaviors; promote involvement in infant care.

Monitor maternal emotional state, as well as maternal-infant interaction.

Encourage and answer questions; share resources for support following discharge.

Problem: Adult Inpatient Plan of Care

Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry.

Maintain adequate skin hydration.

Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based on patient-specific risk factors.

Match turning and repositioning schedule to clinical condition.

Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition if unable to complete independently. Float heels off bed. Avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon.

Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices.

Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.

Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated.

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Intrapartum Bleeding (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Facilitate position change, if possible, to enhance maternal circulation and increase uterine

blood flow (lateral position with hip wedge).

Maintain large-bore intravenous access; provide fluid resuscitation as indicated by maternal status.

Continue fetal heart rate monitoring until delivery.

Note presence of bleeding when membranes are ruptured; test for presence of fetal blood. Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL).

Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed.

Administer oxygen therapy in the presence of maternal hypoxia.

Prepare for expedited delivery with change in maternal or fetal status.

Prepare for uterotonic medication administration for bleeding management following delivery. Anticipate need for further surgical intervention with uncontrolled bleeding (e.g., uterine

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Intrapartum Bleeding (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 01/30/21 Description: Facilitate position change, if possible, to enhance maternal circulation and increase uterine

blood flow (lateral position with hip wedge).

Maintain large-bore intravenous access; provide fluid resuscitation as indicated by maternal status.

Continue fetal heart rate monitoring until delivery.

Note presence of bleeding when membranes are ruptured; test for presence of fetal blood. Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL).

Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed.

Administer oxygen therapy in the presence of maternal hypoxia.

Prepare for expedited delivery with change in maternal or fetal status.

Prepare for uterotonic medication administration for bleeding management following delivery. Anticipate need for surgical intervention with uncontrolled bleeding (e.g., uterine compression sutures, vessel ligation or embolization, hysterectomy).

Maintain calm, reassuring approach with patient and family; answer questions; provide information and timely responses to assist with anxiety reduction.

Problem: Breastfeeding

Resolved: 02/02/21 Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Dates: Disciplines:

Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL).

Verify bladder has been emptied; encourage voiding, perform catheterization if unable to void. Anticipate need for vaginal exam to determine presence of lacerations or hematoma.

Watch for changes in blood pressure, heart rate and urine output.

Anticipate administration of uterotonic medication.

Prepare for additional bleeding-control measures (e.g., balloon tamponade, vaginal packing). Anticipate rapid infusion of intravenous fluid and blood product administration.

Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed.

Monitor for coagulopathy and signs of abnormal bleeding (e.g., oozing from intravenous sites, petechiae).

Anticipate need for surgical intervention with uncontrolled bleeding (e.g., uterine compression sutures, vessel ligation or embolization, hysterectomy).

Maintain calm, reassuring approach with patient and family; answer questions; provide information and timely responses to assist with anxiety reduction.

Goal: Effective Breastfeeding (Resolved)

Dates: Disciplines: Outcomes Date/Time

02/02/21 1053 02/01/21 2357

End: 02/02/21 Nurse, Interdisciplinary

User Outcome Meyer, Mary J, RN Met Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Promote Breast Care and Comfort (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Per CPG

End: 02/02/21

Optimize nipple and skin integrity; avoid use of alcohol or soap solutions; utilize topical agents such as colostrum, breast milk.

Mutually establish acceptable comfort level; evaluate pain using consistent tool or self-report description.

Encourage use of nonpharmacologic comfort measures, such as warm or cold applications, breast massage, manual expression of milk and use of well-fitting support bra; offer analgesia if needed.

Monitor and manage signs of breast or nipple infection; advocate for treatment of mother and infant if nipple is infected.

Encourage continued breastfeeding or breast pumping, even when pain, engorgement, blocked duct or an infection is present; provide reassurance and guidance.

Encourage adequate maternal hydration, nutrition and rest.

Intervention: Promote Effective Breastfeeding (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM

Per CPG

End: 02/02/21

Mutually develop a feeding plan; include father/partner/support person.

Promote cue-based and infant-led feeding; goal of 8 to 12 times per 24-hour period.

Provide ongoing support during feeding sessions to enhance maternal infant experience. Adjust environment to provide calm surroundings and privacy; limit distractions.

Assist with recognition of and response to infant feeding readiness and satiety cues; demonstrate infant rooting and sucking reflexes.

Demonstrate techniques to maintain infant's feeding readiness state, such as unwrapping and gentle tactile or vocal stimulation.

Assist with effective positioning, latch and breast attachment techniques (e.g., move infant to breast versus breast to infant, stimulate suck with drop of colostrum or breast milk).

Observe feedings at minimum twice daily, assessing infant’s ability to latch, suck and swallow, mother infant positioning and presence of milk transfer; evaluate infant for signs of adequate intake (e.g., number of wet diapers and stools).

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1034], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

Intervention: Support Exclusive Breastfeeding Success (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Discuss and reinforce benefits for infant and mother.

Initiate uninterrupted breastfeeding within 1 hour of birth; promote skin-to-skin contact. Advocate for patience and persistence; encourage breastfeeding in the presence of difficult circumstances; eliminate or minimize separation of infant-mother and encourage rooming-in. Initiate pumping of breast milk within 6 hours of birth if unable to nurse.

Assist with breast pumping, proper handling and storage of breast milk.

Advocate for the use of alternative breast milk feeding methods when breastfeeding is medically not possible (e.g., cup, finger, feeding tube device).

Discourage use of a pacifier until breastfeeding is well-established.

Assess support system integrity; emphasize the importance of father/partner/support person involvement and use of available resources.

Discuss future feeding plans and assist with barrier resolution (e.g., return to work, birth control).

Problem: Change in Fetal Wellbeing (Cesarean Delivery)

Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Stable Fetal Wellbeing (Resolved) Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/31/21 0638 Culver, Amy Christine, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2049 Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2012 Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Promote and Monitor Fetal Wellbeing (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Evaluate fetal heart rate tracing.

Facilitate maternal lateral position change to improve uteroplacental blood flow and maternal blood pressure, utilize hip wedge as needed.

Monitor uterine contraction pattern; assess for presence of tachysystole.

Anticipate need for tocolytic administration if tachysystole persists.

Prepare for intravenous fluid bolus administration to improve maternal fluid status and treat hypotension.

Administer oxygen therapy for maternal hypoxia.

Prepare for expedited delivery if fetal status does not improve.

Intervention: Promote and Monitor Fetal Wellbeing (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 01/30/21 Description: Evaluate fetal heart rate tracing.

Facilitate maternal lateral position change to improve uteroplacental blood flow and maternal blood pressure; utilize hip wedge as needed.

Monitor uterine contraction pattern; assess for presence of tachysystole.

Prepare to discontinue oxytocin or labor induction agent in the presence of tachysystole, as applicable.

Anticipate need for tocolytic administration if tachysystole persists.

Prepare for intravenous fluid bolus administration to improve maternal fluid status and treat hypotension.

Administer oxygen therapy for maternal hypoxia.

Review maternal medication history for possible impact on fetal heart rate tracing (e.g., corticosteroid).

Prepare for additional procedure, as indicated, to improve fetal wellbeing (e.g., amnioinfusion, fetal stimulation).

Modify pushing effort, if in second stage of labor.

Prepare for expedited delivery if fetal status does not improve.

Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing Culver, Amy Christine, RN Ongoing, Progressing Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Maintain Anesthesia/Analgesia Safety (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Per CPG

End: 02/02/21

Optimize patient position for insertion accuracy (e.g., sitting, lateral decubitus); consider hip wedge to displace uterus and avoid vessel compression.

Maintain fetal heart rate monitoring.

Implement safety measures (e.g., verify solution/flow rate/pump setting when infusion starts, dose changes or shift changes, use portless infusion tubing, use pumps that prevent free-flow, recheck/trace tubing connections, label tubing).

Prepare for intravenous fluid bolus administration prior to initiation of anesthesia to avoid hypotension.

Prepare for induction of neuraxial anesthesia/analgesia agent; advocate for test dose and anticipate reversal agent.

Maintain supportive environment; promote relaxation (e.g., offer reassurance, provide information, answer questions).

Anticipate urgent medical management for hypotension, block at high level or medication toxicity.

Verify intact catheter tip with removal.

Problem: Infection (Anesthesia/Analgesia, Neuraxial)

Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Absence of Infection Signs and Symptoms (Resolved) Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/31/21 0638 Culver, Amy Christine, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2049 Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2012 Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Infection (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Per CPG

End: 02/02/21

Protect and secure insertion site; observe for signs of infection, sepsis or line dislodgement. Provide meticulous dressing and catheter care to decrease risk of infection; maintain closed system.

Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance. Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g., wound, lines, devices).

Obtain cultures prior to initiating antimicrobial therapy. Do not delay treatment for laboratory results in the presence of high suspicion.

Administer ordered antimicrobial therapy promptly; reassess need regularly.

Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures.

Note: Maternal fever can occur during neuraxial analgesia/anesthesia that is not associated with infection. Source of temperature elevation should be investigated.

Goal: Absence of Infection Signs and Symptoms (Resolved)

Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes ___Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/31/21 0638 Culver, Amy Christine, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2049 Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2012 Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Minimize Infection Risk (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Verify Group B streptococcus status and presence of known infection.

Prepare to administer antimicrobial therapy prophylaxis within 60 minutes prior to delivery, unless there is current coverage provided by existing antimicrobial therapy regimen.

Note: If cesarean delivery is urgently needed, prophylaxis should be administered as soon as possible following incision.

Assist with obtaining cultures, as indicated.

Limit digital vaginal exams or invasive vaginal procedures, especially after membranes are ruptured.

Notify neonatal team for delivery.

Prepare to send placenta for histology following delivery, as indicated.

Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures, as needed.

Assist with obtaining cultures, as indicated.

Limit digital vaginal exams or invasive vaginal procedures, especially after membranes are ruptured.

Prepare to initiate antimicrobial therapy for suspected or confirmed intraamniotic infection. Promote perineal hygiene.

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Infection (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Problem: Labor Pain (Labor)

Resolved: 01/30/21 Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Dates: Disciplines:

Per CPG

End: 02/02/21

Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep, oxygenation, glycemic control and body temperature to maximize resistance.

Maintain dressing and closed drainage system integrity to reduce the risk for infection. Consider splinting techniques to prevent incision dehiscence.

Discontinue prophylactic antimicrobial agent within 24 hours after surgery, as directed. Identify potential sources of infection early; evaluate continued need and advocate for early removal (e.g., lines, devices, drains).

Implement transmission-based precautions and isolation, as indicated, to prevent spread of infection.

If infection suspected, administer ordered antimicrobial therapy promptly; reassess need regularly.

Note: If endometritis is suspected, treatment may be initiated without obtaining cultures. Identify and manage signs of early sepsis.

Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures.

Goal: Acceptable Pain Control (Resolved)

Dates: Disciplines: Outcomes

Date/Time

01/30/21 2047 01/30/21 2012 01/30/21 0511 01/29/21 1848 01/29/21 1456 01/29/21 0517

Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing Tharp, Heather K, RN Ongoing, Progressing Janes, Carrye D, RN Ongoing, Progressing Sylvia, Kayla, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Pain (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM

Per CPG

End: 01/30/21

Determine pain management plan with patient and caregiver; review plan regularly.

Use a consistent, validated tool for pain assessment; evaluate pain level, effect of treatment and patient’s response at regular intervals; along with standard pain scales, the patient can also be asked how she is coping with the pain.

Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.

Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety measures.

Individualize pharmacologic pain management plan; titrate medication to patient response. Prepare for initiation of analgesia/anesthetic, such as neuraxial or inhaled; patient-controlled neuraxial analgesia may be used.

Monitor and address medication-induced effects, such as constipation, nausea, vomiting,

Problem: Pain (Postpartum Cesarean Delivery)

Dates: Disciplines:

Resolved: 02/02/21 Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Goal: Acceptable Pain Control (Resolved)

Dates: Disciplines: Outcomes Date/Time

02/02/21 1053 02/01/21 2357

User Outcome Meyer, Mary J, RN Met Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Pain (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Problem: Perinatal Fall

Dates: Disciplines:

Per CPG

End: 02/02/21

Develop mutual pain management plan with patient and caregiver; review regularly.

Monitor for the presence of incisional pain; provide timely pain management interventions when present.

Use cold application, as culturally-appropriate, to the incisional area for the first 24 to 48 hours to enhance comfort.

Encourage early ambulation, when able, to help avoid gas accumulation which can add to discomfort.

Verify correct infant latch when breastfeeding to prevent nipple pain.

Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.

Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety measures.

Individualize pharmacologic pain management plan; titrate medication to patient response. Combine multimodal analgesia and nonpharmacologic strategies to help potentiate synergistic effects and enhance comfort (e.g., complementary therapy, diversional activity).

Offer around-the-clock dosing of pain medication to keep pain levels in control.

Manage medication-induced effects, such as constipation, nausea, vomiting.

Support and optimize psychosocial response to pain.

If engorgement occurs, encourage more frequent breastfeeding or pumping and storing additional milk to ease discomfort.

Note: Cold compresses, as culturally—appropriate, may be used if bottle-feeding.

If post-dural puncture headache identified, encourage adequate hydration and anticipate the need for epidural blood patch.

If hemorrhoids are present and painful, offer topical pain relief and sitz baths for comfort.

Intervention: Identify and Manage Contributors to Fall Injury Risk (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Reassess fall risk frequently and with change in status or transfer to another level of care.

Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team. (e.g., rounds change of shift/provider, patient transport).

Anticipate needs; perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment, personal needs (e.g., toileting) and placement of necessary items.

Promote use of personal vision and auditory aids (e.g., glasses, hearing aids).

Assess assistance level required for safe and effective self-care; provide support as needed (e.g., toileting, mobilization).

Define behavior and activity limits to patient and family to decrease fall or drop risk.

If fall occurs, assess for and treat injury; determine cause; revise fall injury prevention plan. Regularly review medication contribution to fall risk; consider polypharmacy and high-risk medications (e.g., antiepileptic, narcotic, regional block, anesthesia, sedation) given within last 24 hours.

Adjust medication administration times to minimize risk of falling.

Balance adequate pain management with potential for oversedation

Intervention: Promote Injury-Free Environment (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Provide a safe, barrier-free environment that encourages independent activity.

Keep care area uncluttered and well-lighted.

Determine need for increased observation or auditory alerts (e.g., bed or chair alarm). Assess equipment and environmental modification needs (e.g., low bed, signage, nonskid footwear, grab bars).

Avoid use of restraints.

Intervention: Prevent Newborn Drop or Fall (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG

Dates: End: 02/02/21

Description: Encourage parent/caregiver to use caution if sleepy or sedated when holding and feeding infant.

Facilitate safe transport in bassinet/crib. Promote cautious handling of wet, active infant during bathing. Initiate frequent checks during parental holding and feeding, especially at night.

Problem: Postoperative Urinary Retention (Postpartum Cesarean Delivery)

Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Effective Urinary Elimination (Resolved) Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Urinary Retention (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Monitor fluid balance to promote optimal hydration.

Assess for bladder distension; encourage voiding within 6 hours following removal of indwelling catheter and regularly thereafter.

Promote behavioral methods to enhance voiding ability (e.g., utilize relaxation techniques, mutually establish a regular elimination schedule, allow time for bladder emptying, position to optimize flow, encourage double voiding).

Implement methods to facilitate elimination (e.g., running water, warm water over perineum, sitz bath, privacy).

Utilize portable bladder ultrasound to assess pre- and post-void volumes.

Anticipate the need for intermittent catheterization; advocate for early removal of indwelling device.

Promote urogenital hygiene to prevent skin breakdown.

Promote early mobilization to improve return of urinary tract function.

Problem: Respiratory Compromise (Anesthesia/Analgesia, Neuraxial)

Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Effective Oxygenation and Ventilation (Resolved) Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/31/21 0638 Culver, Amy Christine, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2049 Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2012 Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Optimize Oxygenation and Ventilation (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Use a validated screening tool to identify risk for obstructive sleep apnea prior to placement;

monitor for signs of hypoventilation. Implement continuous pulse oximetry to detect apnea-related oxygen desaturation events.

Maintain patent airway; assess need for additional respiratory support.

Elevate head of bed and position to minimize risk of ventilation/perfusion mismatch, airway collapse/obstruction and aspiration.

Stimulate patient to increase arousal and respiratory effort.

Encourage pulmonary hygiene such as cough-enhancement and airway-clearance techniques (e.g., incentive spirometry, deep breathing, cough).

Provide oxygen therapy judiciously, if hypoxemia is present.

Problem: Skin Injury Risk Increased

Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Goal: Skin Health and Integrity (Resolved) Dates: End: 02/02/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Outcomes Date/Time User Outcome 02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met 02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/31/21 0638 Culver, Amy Christine, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2049 Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing 01/30/21 2012 Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Optimize Skin Protection (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Reassess skin injury risk and inspect skin frequently (e.g., scheduled interval, with turning, with

change in condition) to provide optimal prevention.

Maintain adequate tissue perfusion (e.g., encourage fluid balance, avoid crossing legs, constrictive clothing or devices) to promote tissue oxygenation.

Maintain head of bed at lowest degree of elevation tolerated, considering medical condition and other restrictions. Limit amount of time head of bed is elevated greater than 30 degrees to prevent friction/shear injury.

Avoid positioning onto an area that remains reddened.

Minimize incontinence and moisture (e.g., toileting schedule; moisture-wicking pad, diaper or incontinence collection device, skin moisture barrier).

Cleanse skin promptly and gently when soiled utilizing a pH-balanced cleanser.

Relieve and redistribute pressure (e.g., schedule position changes; utilize higher specification foam mattress, chair cushion, constant low-pressure or alternating-pressure support surface; medical device repositioning; protective dressing applicatio

Support increased progressive functional activity (e.g., therapeutic exercise) to decrease risk associated with immobility. Balance rest with activity.

Intervention: Promote and Optimize Oral Intake (Completed)

Frequency: Per CPG Dates: End: 02/02/21 Description: Assess need and provide assistance with meal set-up and feeding.

Adjust diet or meal schedule based on preferences and tolerance.

Minimize unnecessary dietary restrictions to increase oral intake.

Offer oral supplemental foods or drinks to enhance calorie and protein intake. Establish bowel elimination program to increase comfort and appetite. Provide and encourage oral hygiene to enhance desire to eat.

Consider nutrition support if oral intake remains inadequate.

Robb, Amber, RN Ongoing, Progressing Hicks, Kara, RN Ongoing, Progressing Tharp, Heather K, RN Ongoing, Progressing Janes, Carrye D, RN Ongoing, Progressing Sylvia, Kayla, RN Ongoing, Progressing

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Uterine Activity (Completed)

Frequency: Dates: Description:

Care Plan Timemark History

Per CPG

End: 01/30/21

Monitor uterine contraction activity and fetal heart rate during labor process.

Facilitate maternal position change to lateral with hip wedge as necessary.

Prepare to administer intravenous fluid bolus.

Anticipate need to adjust or discontinue pharmacologic therapy (e.g., cervical ripening agent, oxytocin).

Prepare for oxygen administration in the presence of maternal hypoxia.

Prepare to administer tocolytic agent if uterine activity fails to normalize.

Reassess uterine activity and fetal heart rate for response to interventions.

Prepare for delivery if contraction pattern or fetal heart rate fail to normalize or with change in maternal-fetal status.

Notify neonatal team of delivery.

Point: Infection Prevention (Resolved)

Description: Both healthcare team members and patients can help stop the spread of infection.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 353




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1004], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-1003] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

Patient Education (continued)

Staff and visitors should use hand cleanser or wash their hands when entering and exiting a room, as well as prior to and after touching the patient.

Visitors should wear proper protection when infection-prevention signs are posted.

Speak up if infection prevention principles are not practiced by patients, visitors or healthcare team members.

Friends and family with symptoms of illness should avoid visiting.

Educate patients and family members about the importance of daily hygiene care and special handling of drains, wounds and dressings.

Patient Friendly Description: Everyone, including you, can stop the spread of infection.

Staff and visitors should clean their hands before and after contact with your child/you.

Everyone should clean their hands when coming in and leaving your child's/your room.

Your child/you should wash your hands often and after going to the bathroom.

Special items may need to be worn by anyone coming into your child's/your room, such as a mask, gown and gloves. Friends and family who are sick should not visit.

Speak up if anyone is not following these rules. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back, Verbalizes Understanding by KS at 1/29/2021 0517

Significant Other Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back, Verbalizes Understanding by KS at 1/29/2021 0517

Point: Harm Prevention (Resolved)

Description: Inform the patient and family that there are several ways to make sure the right care is received. Safety measures include: patient identification verified using 2 identifiers barcode scanning medication reconciliation IV lines and tubing tracing Encourage the patient and family to speak up with any concerns they have about care.

Patient Friendly Description: The healthcare team uses many ways to make sure that your child/you are getting the right care.

Your child/you will be asked to tell name and birth date many times.

Your child's/your identification bracelet may be scanned as well. This helps to make sure that the right treatments are given to the right patient.

If your child/you have any IV lines or tubing, we will be checking them often.

Please tell someone if you have questions or concerns about your child's/your care.

Point: Pain Assessment Process (Resolved)

Description: Determine the patient's preferred pain scale and discuss planned reassessment schedule.

It is important that pain is managed, so that the patient can be an active participant in the treatment and therapy needed for his/her recovery.

Inform the patient and family that managing pain is a partnership. It is important to speak up if pain is not well-controlled.

Patient Friendly Description: It is important that your child/you are comfortable so that activities important to your health and healing can be performed.

Do not wait until your child's/your pain is bad to ask for help. Learning Progress Summary

Title: Perinatal Fall Injury Risk Education (Obstetrics) () Topic: Overview (Resolved) Point: Risk Factors (Resolved)

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 359




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-992], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-991] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

Description: Risk factors may include: change in balance, gait, mobility due to pregnancy weight gain, obesity fatigue and/or sleep disturbance hypotension inappropriate footwear prolonged bedrest history of fall medication (e.g., polypharmacy; sedative, hypnotic, laxative, diuretic, antihyperglycemic, cardiac) depression anxiety fear of falling impaired vision, hearing environmental factor (e.g., inadequate lighting, slippery or uneven surfaces, clutter, hard to reach objects, stairs without railing, unfamiliar surroundings) neurologic disease injury that affects gait, mobility

Patient Friendly Description: Risk factors may include:

poor balance due to pregnancy weight gain

the size of your belly (uterus)

being unable to see your feet

wearing shoes with high heels or flip-flops

having fallen in the past

trouble sleeping, feeling tired

effect of medicine you are taking

being afraid of falling or losing balance

having a sickness or injury that causes dizziness or weakness

not being able to see or hear well

having dangers at home, such as poor lighting, slippery or uneven floors, clutter having to reach high or bend low to get what you need

taking the stairs

Standing up too fast may make your blood pressure drop and cause you to feel dizzy.

Being on bedrest for a long time can cause your muscles to get weak. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back, Verbalizes Understanding by KS at 1/29/2021 0517

Description: Medication can cause dizziness, contributing to risk for falling. These include: CNS, antipsychotic and cardiovascular medications.

Patient Friendly Description: Some medicine can put you at a greater risk for falling.

Take the medicine just as the doctor tells you.

Call the doctor if there are any side effects from taking the medicine. Learning Progress Summary

Point: Safety (Resolved) Description: Safe shoes should be worn (e.g., low heels; no flip flops). Caution should be used while walking, especially while carrying a baby or a large load; watch for trip danger. Use caution getting in or out of the bath or shower; have a support person nearby.

Patient Friendly Description: Add fresh light bulbs, night lights, take up throw rugs, add railings to stairs, pick up clutter.

Wear safe shoes such as low heels or sneakers and not flip-flops. Watch for trip danger when walking, especially when your baby is in your arms. Be careful getting in and out of the shower or bath.

Have a person close by in case you need help. Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Topic: CAUTI: Urinary Catheter Placement (Resolved)

Point: Procedure Review (Resolved)

Description: A urinary catheter is a flexible tube passed through the urethra and into the bladder to drain urine into a collection device.

Patient Friendly Description: A catheter is a soft tube that is placed in the bladder to drain pee (urine).

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Point: Indications (Resolved)

Description:

Indications for catheter placement include: urinary retention or bladder outlet obstruction obstruction accurate measurement of urine in critically ill patient prolonged surgical procedure

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 362




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-809], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-985] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

receiving large volumes of infusions or diuretics during surgery medications that increase urine output

open sacral or perineal wounds with incontinence

required prolonged immobilization

select genitourinary surgical procedures

required intraoperative monitoring of urinary output

Patient Friendly Description: A drain may be put in your bladder because: All the urine won't come out when you pee. Something is stopping or blocking the pee from coming out. A test is needed to find out how well your kidneys or bladder are working. You need to find out how much you are peeing. You are having a long surgery or test. You can't get up to the bathroom to pee (immobility). You have open sores in your private area that need to heal.

Topic: CAUTI: Catheter-Associated Infection (Resolved)

Point: Signs/Symptoms (Resolved)

Description: Signs/symptoms of a catheter associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) may include: fatigue fever flank pain cloudy, foul-smelling urine hematuria suprapubic tenderness urinary frequency urinary urgency dysuria

Patient Friendly Description: Signs you may have an infection include: feeling very tired fever pain in your lower back or tummy (stomach) pain or burning when you pee (pass urine) pee (urine) smells bad or looks dark or bloody you need to pee (pass urine) more often or can’t hold it for very long Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Point: When to Seek Medical Attention (Resolved)

Description: After the catheter is removed, the provider (specify) should be called if symptoms new infection symptoms develop Immediate medical care should be sought if: unable to void fever symptoms become worse

Patient Friendly Description:

An infection can start while you have a tube in your bladder.

It could start in the days after your tube is taken out. Call the doctor if you start to have infection symptoms or are not getting better. Call your doctor right away if you can't pee (pass urine) or have a fever.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 363




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-811], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

Patient Education (continued) Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Point: Description (Resolved)

Description: A catheter associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) is an infection related to invasive device placement such as a urinary catheter.

Risk of infection is directly related to length of time the catheter is in place.

Patient Friendly Description: A catheter associated urinary tract infection (CAUTI) is an infection during or after a tube is in your bladder. The infection can be in any part of your urinary system. It could be in your kidneys. It could be in the tubes that carry pee (urine) from your kidneys to the bladder. It could be in your bladder. It could be in the tube that carries pee (urine) from your bladder to outside of your body. You are more likely to get an infection the longer you have the tube.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Point: Infection Prevention (Resolved)

Description: The patient should know the catheter will not be left in any longer than needed. The tube will be: secured to thigh checked for kinks often The drainage bag will be: emptied and measured often hung below the level of hips, but never on the floor The care team and should will: wash hands before and after catheter care provide perineal care at least daily and after bowel movements

Patient Friendly Description: Taking the tube out as soon as you don't need it will help. Other ways to prevent infection include: keep the tube fastened to your upper leg to keep it from being pulled out by mistake make sure it drains well keep the drain bag below your hips and off the floor It will be emptied often to check how much urine is in the bag. Everyone should wash their hands before and after touching your tube. Your private area should be washed at least once a day and after you poop.

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AR at 1/30/2021 2012

Title: Postpartum Cesarean Delivery Education (Obstetrics) () Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)

Point: Self-Care (Resolved)

Description: Self-care following a cesarean delivery includes: incision care Ambulating soon after delivery promotes healing and improves well-being. When getting up from bed, the patient should sit briefly and then stand without walking for a few seconds. This will help to prevent dizziness or lightheadedness. Good posture should be maintained when walking to take pressure off her back. It can take several weeks to feel fully physically recovered. Encourage rest each day and to gradually increase activity. Suggest napping when the infant naps. Visitors and social activities should be limited. Strenuous work should be avoided. Lifting should be limited to no more than 10 pounds. Help should be sought from family/friends for household chores and child care. Personal hygiene includes: avoid douching and using tampons until care provider says that it is okay use a squirt bottle to rinse her bottom front to back after urinating or having a bowel movement; wiping should also be from front to back showering frequently once care provider has said that it is okay

Patient Friendly Description: It may take several weeks to feel fully recovered after a cesarean delivery.

Getting up and walking right after the delivery is good for healing.

Sit on the edge of the bed before standing so you won't feel dizzy.

Sleep enough so that you feel rested, balancing activity with periods of rest. Take naps when your baby sleeps.

Try to keep friends and family from visiting unless they are there to help. Do not lift anything heavier than 10 pounds.

Avoid exercise until your doctor says that it is safe.

Ask for help with house work or caring for your other children.

Avoid douching or using tampons until your doctor says that is okay.

Use a squirt bottle to rinse your bottom front to back after peeing (urinating) or pooping (having a stool). Wipe from front to back.

It is okay to shower and bathe frequently.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 365




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-980], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-979] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

Learning Progress Summary Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back, Verbalizes Understanding by KP at 2/1/2021 2358 Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Expected Physical Changes (Resolved) Description: Uterine Changes: Menstrual-like cramping is expected as uterine involution takes place. Lochia should progressively decrease in amount; change in color is normal over the next several weeks. Lochia changes from rubra (1 - 3 days) to pinkish-brown and gradually to alba (white) after postpartum day 10 and for up to 6 weeks. It should never have a foul odor (may be a signal of infection). Breast engorgement occurs on postpartum day 2 or 3. If not breastfeeding, breasts should return to pre-pregnant condition within 1 to 2 weeks after delivery. Fluid shifting following delivery may cause bladder over-distension. Voiding may also be affected by anesthetic. Voiding should occur as soon as possible after delivery. If unable to void, straight catheterization may be necessary. With continued difficulty, a temporary indwelling urinary catheter may be necessary. Sweating and more frequent urination occurs due to normal fluid shifts during the first week after delivery. Treatment of Constipation: It is common for bowel movements to be irregular after delivery. Stool softeners can help to make the stool easier to pass. Drink 8-10 8 oz. glasses of water a day. Eat more high-fiber foods, like fruits, vegetables, whole grain breads, cereals and dry beans. A mild laxative may be ordered if no bowel movement has passed in a couple of days.

Patient Friendly Description: You will notice many changes over the next few days as your body returns to a ‘before pregnancy’ state.

Changes include:

Cramps, like what you feel during a period, as your uterus shrinks a little each day.

Bleeding that will gradually slow and stop.

The bleeding (discharge) will also change in color from red to pinkish-brown to white.

The bleeding or discharge should never smell bad.

You will notice your breasts become swollen and firm with breast milk; this is called engorgement.

This happens 2 or 3 days after birth.

If you are not breastfeeding, a snug bra and ice packs may help you feel better until it gradually goes away. It is important that you are able to pee (urinate) soon after your baby is born.

If you have trouble peeing you may have to have a catheter (tube in your bladder) put in for a short time.

You may notice that you are going to the bathroom (to pee) more often or sweating more during the first week after birth. This is normal because your body is getting rid of extra fluid from pregnancy.

It is common to have trouble pooping (having a stool) after your baby is born. Drink a lot of fluids, especially water. Eat foods that are high in fiber like fresh fruits and raw vegetables and whole grain breads and cereals.

Your doctor may suggest medicine to help you go if needed.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 366




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-817], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-818] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

Point: Infant Safety (Resolved)

Description: To prevent accidental dropping or injury, the baby should be placed safely in the bassinette when napping and to sleep at night.

The infant should not be fed unattended if mother is feeling sleepy or if sedated from pain medicine.

Patient Friendly Description: When you sleep, put your baby in the crib. You should never sleep with your baby.

Make sure someone is with you while you feed or care for your baby if you are feeling sleepy or have taken pain medicine. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back, Verbalizes Understanding by KP at 2/1/2021 2358 Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809 Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AC at 1/31/2021 0640

Point: Expected Psychological Changes (Resolved)

Description: During the postpartum period parents begin bonding or feeling attached to their infant. This happens right away for some but may take longer for others. This is normal. Baby blues” are common during the first few weeks after delivery caused in part by hormone changes, sleep deprivation and family lifestyle adjustment. Blues usually resolve within a week after delivery. Postpartum depression is more serious and longer lasting. It is treatable. It is important to seek help. Symptoms of a problem should be reported to the care provider immediately. These include: mood swings extreme anxiety sleep disturbances repetitive/intrusive thoughts/worry about infant’s welfare thoughts of harming self or infant loss of interest in caring for infant fear of being left alone with infant Encourage Identification of a personal support system. Provide information on local postpartum depression support group/behavioral health resource.

Patient Friendly Description: The birth of your baby causes emotions that may be new to you.

Some parents feel a strong attachment to their baby right away. For others it may take longer. Both are 'normal.

Bonding is a process that happens while you are giving your baby the care that he needs every day. You may not even know it is happening until your baby smiles for the first time and you feel great love and joy.

It is very common to feel down or cry easily after your baby is born. This is caused by changes in your hormones, lack of sleep and the hard work of adjusting to a newborn baby. This is called the 'Baby Blues’. You will usually feel better in a week or so.

If the feelings last longer or get worse, you may be having postpartum depression.

If you have any of these symptoms you should call your doctor right away:

changes in your mood from very happy to very sad

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 367




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-819], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-975] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

feeling irritable or cranky

sleeping too little or too much

thoughts of hurting yourself or your baby.

It is important that you reach out for help.

Call your doctor right away.

You can also call a family member or friend for support.

Consider counseling and joining a support group for moms with postpartum depression in your community. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: VTE Prevention (Resolved)

Description: Pregnancy and the postpartum period greatly increase the risk of developing VTE. To prevent VTE, early frequent ambulation is encouraged and should be continued at home. Provider's instructions for continuing VTE prophylaxis medication (as applicable) should be followed at home.

Patient Friendly Description: Being pregnant and delivering a baby can increase your chances of getting a blood clot.

If you have a blood clot, it will most likely be in one of your arms or legs. A blood clot can also travel to your lungs. This is known as a pulmonary embolism.

One of the best things you can do to prevent a blood clot is to get up and walk often. You should also do this after you go home.

If you have been taking special medicine to prevent blood clots, your doctor may want you to keep taking it after you go home. Take this medicine only as your doctor tell you.

Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Incision Care (Resolved) Description: Instruct patient to follow provider's instructions for incision/site care. Hands should be washed with soap and water before and after caring for incision. Generally, the dressing will be removed after 24 hours. The incision should be kept clean and dry by cleansing with soap and water, rinsing and patting dry to prevent infection. Taking a shower or soaking in a tub should be avoided until provider says that it is okay. Underwear or clothing that rubs or places pressure on the area should be avoided. The incision should be exposed to air as much as possible, particularly if it located under a skin flap.

Patient Friendly Description: Follow what your doctor's tells you for how to take care of your incision.

Wash your hands with soap and water before and after taking care of your incision. The dressing will likely be removed after 24 hours. Unless you are told differently, cleanse the incision gently with soap and water, rinse and pat dry.

Do not take a shower or soak in a tub until your doctor tells you it is okay.

Title: Labor Education (Obstetrics) () Topic: Overview (Resolved)

Point: Description (Resolved)

Description: Labor is the process during which the fetus, membranes, umbilical cord and placenta are expelled from the uterus. There are three stages.

First stage:

begins with regular uterine contractions and ends with complete cervical dilatation at 10 cm

Second stage:

begins with complete cervical dilatation and ends with the delivery of the infant

Third stage:

period between the delivery of the infant and the delivery of the placenta and fetal membranes

Patient Friendly Description: Labor is the process of childbirth.

During labor, the baby, bag of water, umbilical cord and placenta are come out of the womb (uterus). There are three parts to labor.

The first begins when labor pains start and ends when you are fully dilated.

The second ends with the baby's birth.

The third part ends after the placenta (afterbirth) is passed.

Point: Signs/Symptoms (Resolved) Description: Signs/symptoms may include: regular contractions that increase in intensity, frequency and duration passing of cervical mucus plug, bloody vaginal discharge (bloody show) discomfort usually beginning as a dull ache in lower back rupture of amniotic membranes pelvic pressure with the urge to push

Patient Friendly Description: Symptoms of labor may include:

pain that starts as a backache

labor pains that are spaced evenly apart

some pink or light red mucus discharge from the birth canal (bloody show)

water breaks

You will notice that the labor pain gets get stronger, lasts longer and gets closer together.

Just before delivery, you may feel pressure and want to push (bear down). Learning Progress Summary

Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)

Point: Labor Care (Resolved)

Description: The care provided during labor is determined by many factors (e.g., gestation, contraction pattern, cervical dilation, presenting symptoms, patient preference/birth plan). If there is a birth plan, it should be reviewed with the patient and family. Contractions will be monitored throughout the labor process. Electronic fetal monitoring will be used to monitor fetal status. Control of pain during labor may be accomplished with a variety of methods (e.g., relaxation and breathing techniques, position changes, analgesia and/or anesthesia). As labor progresses, position changes can be expected. If there are positions that will better facilitate delivery and are not contraindicated, they should be utilized. Support during labor should be encouraged.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 370

[DOB-969] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

Patient Friendly Description: The care you receive in labor depends on many things.

Your doctor or nurse will tell you more about how this applies to you. If you have a birth plan, the healthcare team will be reviewing it with you. It will be followed whenever possible.

There are many ways to help you control pain during labor, like special breathing and relaxing exercises that you learned in childbirth classes. Your nurse will help you find ways that are right for you.

Discuss epidural anesthesia with your doctor if this is something that you would like to try. Learning Progress Summary
