Title: Breastfeeding Education (Adult,Obstetrics) () Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)

Point: Pacifiers and Formula (Resolved)

Description: Pacifier use is linked to breastfeeding difficulties (nipple confusion, poor latch) in full-term infants. Non-nutritive sucking with a pacifier may benefit the preterm infant. Milk substitutes (formula) can lead to decreased breast milk supply and alter the benefits of breast milk for the infant.

Patient Friendly Description: Giving your baby a pacifier when you are breastfeeding can cause him/her to get mixed up. A pacifier nipple feels different than your nipple.

Your baby might not breastfeed as well. Sometimes, when a baby is born early, the doctor will want the baby to use a pacifier. Giving your baby formula instead of breast milk can keep him/her from getting the good things that breast milk provides.

Talk to your baby's doctor before giving your baby a pacifier or formula. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Infant-Led Feeding (Resolved)

Description: Frequent feedings will help the mother make more milk. The infant should be breastfed: when he/she shows signs of being hungry (feeding on demand) to reduce the fullness of his/her mother's breasts until he/she is done (infant-led feeding schedule) The infant should be allowed to feed on each breast for as long as he/she wants. In the first few weeks following birth, the infant may be sleepier and need to be awakened for a feeding. As a guide: newborns may breastfeed every 1 to 3 hours newborns should not go longer than 3 hours without a feeding during the day newborns should not go longer than 5 hours without a feeding during the night

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 372




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-829], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-965] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

Patient Education (continued) infants should be fed at least 8 times in a 24 hour period

Patient Friendly Description: You will make more milk if you feed your baby many times a day.

Breastfeed your baby whenever he/she acts hungry (feeding on demand).

Let your baby feed from both breasts for as long as he/she wants (baby-led feeding schedule).

Your baby may be very sleepy right after he/she is born (first 2 weeks). You may need to wake him/her up for feeding. New babies:

will need to be breastfed every 1 to 3 hours

should not wait longer than 3 hours before being fed during the day

should not wait longer than 5 hours before being fed during the night

Babies should be fed at least 8 times a day (within 24 hours). Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Breast Care (Resolved)

Description: Breast care is important for a positive breastfeeding experience. To prevent nipples from becoming dry, cracked and sore, the mother should: wash her breasts every day avoid using soap on her nipples air-dry her nipples for 3 to 4 minutes after a feeding wear a supportive bra use only cotton bra pads to absorb leaking breast milk use lanolin or expressed breast milk on her nipples after feeding to keep them from getting too dry ask her doctor before using breast creams or breast shells

Patient Friendly Description: Nipples can become dry, cracked and sore without proper care of your breasts. This can have a negative impact on your breastfeeding experience.

Wash your breasts every day.

Try not to use soap on your nipples.

Air-dry your nipples for 3 to 4 minutes after you feed your baby.

Wear a good bra that supports your breasts.

Use only cotton pads in your bra to soak up leaking breast milk.

Use lanolin or expressed breast milk on your nipples after feeding to keep them from getting too dry.

Ask your doctor before using breast creams or breast shells. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Breast Milk Supply (Resolved)

Description: Advise the mother to:

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 373




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-831], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-963] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

drink plenty of fluids and eat 3 healthy meals each day

use breast massage and manual expression (before pumping or breastfeeding)

pump frequently if unable to breastfeed; 8 times a day (every 2 to 3 hours during the day and at least once at night) avoid limiting the length of time for each breastfeeding session

Patient Friendly Description: Drink enough fluid so that (pee) urine is clear pale yellow.

Eat 3 healthy meals each day. Massage your breasts and express milk by hand before you pump or breastfeed.

If you are not able to breastfeed, pump at least 8 times each day. This means every 2 to 3 hours during the day and at least 1 time at night.

Do not limit how long your baby breastfeeds each time. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Infant Positioning (Resolved)

Description: Positioning is important for feeding success. Pillows or blanket rolls should be used for support. The infant should be brought to the breast rather than the mother leaning forward toward the infant. Assist the mother in determining the most comfortable and effective hold for her and the infant (e.g., clutch or football, cradle or across the lap, side-lying, ventral or lying down).

Patient Friendly Description: The way you hold your baby while you are breastfeeding will help him/her eat better. It will make your breasts feel better too.

Use pillows or a blanket under your arm while you are feeding your baby. Bring your baby to your breast, instead of leaning your breast forward toward your baby. You should decide which way to hold your baby during breastfeeding.

Pick a way that makes you and your baby feel the best. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Adequate Intake (Resolved)

Description: Adequate intake of breast milk is measured by the infant's amount of urine and stools: Age 3 to 5 days of life: infant having at least 3 to 5 wet diapers and 3 to 4 stools per 24 hours Age 5 to 7 days of life: infant having at least 4 to 6 wet diapers and 3 to 6 stools per 24 hours

Patient Friendly Description: You can tell if your baby is getting enough to eat by counting his/her wet and dirty diapers.

Age 2 to 5 days: at least 3 to 5 wet diapers every day

at least 3 to 4 dirty diapers every day

[DOB-961] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) Patient Education (continued)

Age 5 to 7 days: at least 4 to 6 wet diapers every day

at least 3 to 6 dirty diapers every day Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Latching/Swallowing (Resolved)

Description: The mother will know the baby has latched effectively by feeling a painless and silent tugging or pulling sensation when the infant takes her breast.

She may hear the infant swallowing between sucks (every 3 to 4 sucks).

She will see muscle movement above and in front of the infant's ears while he/she is sucking.

She should not hear clicking or smacking while the infant is sucking.

She should not have nipple pain while the infant is sucking.

Patient Friendly Description: There are several ways you can tell if your baby is attached to your breast well (latch).

When your baby starts to suck, it should feel like a little tugging or pulling on your breast. It should not hurt. You may hear your baby swallowing between sucks (every 3 to 4 sucks).

You will see movement by your baby's ears while he/she is sucking.

You should not hear clicking or smacking while the baby is sucking.

You should not have nipple pain while your baby is sucking. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding, Demonstrated Understanding by KS1 at 2/1/2021 0809

Point: Fluid/Food Intake (Resolved)

Description: The breastfeeding mother will burn about 400 to 500 additional calories each day. The calories should be replaced by maintaining a healthy diet and drinking enough fluids so that urine output is clear, pale yellow. This will also help to maintain the mother's milk supply.

Some foods may cause the infant to have gas, making him/her more fussy.

Avoid or limit these foods, which include:

chocolate

spicy food

some vegetables, such as broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, onions or Brussels sprouts

Avoid or limit caffeine and alcohol.

Avoid eating fish with high mercury content, such as tilefish, shark, swordfish and king mackerel.

Follow specific instructions from provider.

Patient Friendly Description: When you are breastfeeding, you are burning 400 - 500 more calories per day than normal. These calories should be replaced by eating a healthy diet and drinking enough fluids so that your pee (urine) is clear, pale yellow. This will also help your milk supply.

Some foods will give your baby gas, causing him/her to be fussier.

Avoid or limit these foods, which may include:

chocolate

spicy food

some vegetables, such as broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, onions and Brussels sprouts

Avoid or limit caffeine and alcohol.

Avoid eating fish with high mercury content, such as tilefish, shark, swordfish and king mackerel.

Follow specific diet instructions from your doctor.

ANESTHESIA I understand that this procedure may require anesthesia. An anesthesiology provider has or will explain the potential benefits, risks, side effects, and potential anesthesia alternatives. If your provider recommends and will manage local anesthesia or moderate sedation as noted below, this discussion will occur with your provider. Complications from any typeof anesthetic are infrequent, but do occur. Complications are more frequent for patients in poor health than for patients in good health. Complications associated with all types of anesthesia include temporary or permanent damage to teeth, lips, nose, throat, voice box, windpipe, eyes, nerves, or blood vessels. Drug reaction may occur and may be minor or serious even requiring resuscitation. Breathing problems may develop. Headache, back pain, some degree of awareness, infection, nausea and/or vomiting may occur. There are some very serious complications, which are uncommon, butcan occur. These include paralysis, brain damage, or death. There are other potential complications depending on your situation, however, it is not possible to advise you of every imaginable complication. Serious complications are unlikely. PATIENT ASSESSMENT FOR ADMINISTRATION OF NITROUS OXIDE FOR PAIN MANAGMENT

Nitrous Oxide Assessment

This document serves 4s a preadmission assessment for pain management use of Nitrous Oxide in Labor & Delivery (U&D) at Baptist Health Louisville. The form should be faxed to L&D, or uploaded into EPIC prior to patient admission.

The following medicaljissues are contraindicated in the use of nitrous oxide:

Pernicious anemia or B12 deficiency Daily Methadone or Suboxone use Any concern for fetal status

PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL: Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. Other Instructions

Discharge Instructions

Nothing vaginally until after seen in office. Bath, shower, stairs ok. Driving is ok when the baby is a week old and if you are not taking percocet/narcotics. No intentional exercise or heavy lifting over 10 Ibs. If something hurts, don't do it. Call for heavy vaginal bleeding more than soaking a pad/hour, fever of 101. More Information

Breastfeeding

Areola YW

Choosing to breastfeed is one of the best decisions you can make for yourself and your = = baby. A change in hormones during pregnancy causes your breasts to make breast milk _ jj in your milk-producing glands. Hormones prevent breast milk from being released “Re |

before your baby is born. They also prompt milk flow after birth. Once breastfeeding

has begun, thoughts of your baby, as well as his or her sucking or crying, can stimulate the release of milk from your milk-producing glands.

Flanged lips

Benefits of breastfeeding

Research shows that breastfeeding offers many health benefits for infants and mothers. It also offers a cost-free and convenient way to feed your baby.

For your baby

e Your first milk (colostrum) helps your baby's digestive system to function better.

e Special cells in your milk (antibodies) help your baby to fight off infections.

e Breastfed babies are less likely to develop asthma, allergies, obesity, or type 2 diabetes. They are also at lower risk for sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).

e Nutrients in breast milk are better able to meet your baby’s needs compared to infant formula.

e Breast milk improves your baby's brain development.

For you

Breastfeeding helps to create a very special bond between you and your baby.

Breastfeeding is convenient. Breast milk costs nothing and is always available at the correct temperature. Breastfeeding helps to burn calories. It helps you to lose the weight that you gained during pregnancy. Breastfeeding makes your uterus return faster to its size before pregnancy. It also slows bleeding (/ochia) after you give birth.

e Breastfeeding helps to lower your risk of developing type 2 diabetes, osteoporosis, rheumatoid arthritis, cardiovascular disease, and breast, ovarian, uterine, and endometrial cancer later in life.

Breastfeeding basics Starting breastfeeding

e Find a comfortable place to sit or lie down, with your neck and back well-supported.

e Place a pillow or a rolled-up blanket under your baby to bring him or her to the level of your breast (if you are seated). Nursing pillows are specially designed to help support your arms and your baby while you breastfeed.

e Make sure that your baby's tummy (abdomen) is facing your abdomen.

e Gently massage your breast. With your fingertips, massage from the outer edges of your breast inward toward the nipple. This encourages milk flow. If your milk flows slowly, you may need to continue this action during the feeding.

e Support your breast with 4 fingers underneath and your thumb above your nipple (make the letter "C" with your hand). Make sure your fingers are well away from your nipple and your baby’s mouth.

e Stroke your baby's lips gently with your finger or nipple.

e When your baby's mouth is open wide enough, quickly bring your baby to your breast, placing your entire nipple and as much of the areola as possible into your baby's mouth. The areola is the colored area around your nipple.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 390




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-881], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-882] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued) © More areola should be visible above your baby's upper lip than below the lower lip. °o Your baby's lips should be opened and extended outward (flanged) to ensure an adequate, comfortable latch.

o Your baby's tongue should be between his or her lower gum and your breast.

e Make sure that your baby's mouth is correctly positioned around your nipple (/atched). Your baby's lips should

create a seal on your breast and be turned out (everted). e Itis common for your baby to suck about 2—3 minutes in order to start the flow of breast milk.

Latching

Teaching your baby how to latch onto your breast properly is very important. An improper latch can cause nipple pain, decreased milk supply, and poor weight gain in your baby. Also, if your baby is not latched onto your nipple properly, he or she may swallow some air during feeding. This can make your baby fussy. Burping your baby when you switch breasts during the feeding can help to get rid of the air. However, teaching your baby to latch on properly is still the best way to prevent fussiness from swallowing air while breastfeeding.

Signs that your baby has successfully latched onto your nipple

e Silent tugging or silent sucking, without causing you pain. Infant's lips should be extended outward (flanged).

e Swallowing heard between every 3-4 sucks once your milk has started to flow (after your let-down milk reflex occurs).

e Muscle movement above and in front of his or her ears while sucking.

Signs that your baby has not successfully latched onto your nipple e Sucking sounds or smacking sounds from your baby while breastfeeding. e Nipple pain.

If you think your baby has not latched on correctly, slip your finger into the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the suction and place it between your baby's gums. Attempt to start breastfeeding again.

Signs of successful breastfeeding

Signs from your baby

Your baby will gradually decrease the number of sucks or will completely stop sucking. Your baby will fall asleep.

Your baby's body will relax.

Your baby will retain a small amount of milk in his or her mouth.

Your baby will let go of your breast by himself or herself.

Signs from you

e Breasts that have increased in firmness, weight, and size 1—3 hours after feeding.

e Breasts that are softer immediately after breastfeeding.

e Increased milk volume, as well as a change in milk consistency and color by the fifth day of breastfeeding. e Nipples that are not sore, cracked, or bleeding.

Signs that your baby is getting enough milk

e Wetting at least 1-2 diapers during the first 24 hours after birth.

e Wetting at least 5—6 diapers every 24 hours for the first week after birth. The urine should be clear or pale yellow by the age of 5 days.

e Wetting 6-8 diapers every 24 hours as your baby continues to grow and develop.

e At least 3 stools in a 24-hour period by the age of 5 days. The stool should be soft and yellow.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 391




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-883], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-911] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued) At least 3 stools in a 24-hour period by the age of 7 days. The stool should be seedy and yellow.

No loss of weight greater than 10% of birth weight during the first 3 days of life.

Average weight gain of 4-7 oz (113-198 g) per week after the age of 4 days.

Consistent daily weight gain by the age of 5 days, without weight loss after the age of 2 weeks.

After a feeding, your baby may spit up a small amount of milk. This is normal. Breastfeeding frequency and duration

Frequent feeding will help you make more milk and can prevent sore nipples and extremely full breasts (breast engorgement). Breastfeed when you feel the need to reduce the fullness of your breasts or when your baby shows signs of hunger. This is called "breastfeeding on demand." Signs that your baby is hungry include:

Increased alertness, activity, or restlessness.

Movement of the head from side to side.

Opening of the mouth when the corner of the mouth or cheek is stroked (rooting). Increased sucking sounds, smacking lips, cooing, sighing, or squeaking. Hand-to-mouth movements and sucking on fingers or hands.

Fussing or crying.

Avoid introducing a pacifier to your baby in the first 4-6 weeks after your baby is born. After this time, you may choose to use a pacifier. Research has shown that pacifier use during the first year of a baby's life decreases the risk of sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).

Allow your baby to feed on each breast as long as he or she wants. When your baby unlatches or falls asleep while feeding from the first breast, offer the second breast. Because newborns are often sleepy in the first few weeks of life, you may need to awaken your baby to get him or her to feed.

Breastfeeding times will vary from baby to baby. However, the following rules can serve as a guide to help you make sure that your baby is properly fed:

e Newborns (babies 4 weeks of age or younger) may breastfeed every 1-3 hours. e Newborns should not go without breastfeeding for longer than 3 hours during the day or 5 hours during the night. e You should breastfeed your baby a minimum of 8 times in a 24-hour period.

Breast milk pumping

Electric breast pump

#

Pumping and storing breast milk allows you to make sure that your baby is exclusively fed

your breast milk, even at times when you are unable to breastfeed. This is especially important tee if you go back to work while you are still breastfeeding, or if you are not able to be present during tae fg feedings. Your lactation consultant can help you find a method of pumping that works best for you

and give you guidelines about how long it is safe to store breast milk.

Caring for your breasts while you breastfeed Nipples can become dry, cracked, and sore while breastfeeding. The following recommendations can help keep your breasts moisturized and healthy:

e Avoid using soap on your nipples. e Wear a supportive bra designed especially for nursing. Avoid wearing underwire-style bras or extremely tight Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 392




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-885], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-886] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued) bras (sports bras). e Air-dry your nipples for 3—4 minutes after each feeding. e Use only cotton bra pads to absorb leaked breast milk. Leaking of breast milk between feedings is normal. e Use lanolin on your nipples after breastfeeding. Lanolin helps to maintain your skin's normal moisture barrier. Pure lanolin is not harmful (not toxic) to your baby. You may also hand express a few drops of breast milk and gently massage that milk into your nipples and allow the milk to air-dry.

In the first few weeks after giving birth, some women experience breast engorgement. Engorgement can make your breasts feel heavy, warm, and tender to the touch. Engorgement peaks within 3—5 days after you give birth. The following recommendations can help to ease engorgement:

e Completely empty your breasts while breastfeeding or pumping. You may want to start by applying warm, moist heat (in the shower or with warm, water-soaked hand towels) just before feeding or pumping. This increases circulation and helps the milk flow. If your baby does not completely empty your breasts while breastfeeding, pump any extra milk after he or she is finished.

e Apply ice packs to your breasts immediately after breastfeeding or pumping, unless this is too uncomfortable for you. To do this:

© Put ice in a plastic bag. o Place a towel between your skin and the bag. © Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. e Make sure that your baby is latched on and positioned properly while breastfeeding.

If engorgement persists after 48 hours of following these recommendations, contact your health care provider or a lactation consultant.

Overall health care recommendations while breastfeeding

e Eat 3 healthy meals and 3 snacks every day. Well-nourished mothers who are breastfeeding need an additional 450-500 calories a day. You can meet this requirement by increasing the amount of a balanced diet that you eat.

e Drink enough water to keep your urine pale yellow or clear.

e Rest often, relax, and continue to take your prenatal vitamins to prevent fatigue, stress, and low vitamin and mineral levels in your body (nutrient deficiencies).

e Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. Your baby may be harmed by chemicals from cigarettes that pass into breast milk and exposure to secondhand smoke. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider.

e Avoid alcohol.

e Do not use illegal drugs or marijuana.

e Talk with your health care provider before taking any medicines. These include over-the-counter and prescription medicines as well as vitamins and herbal supplements. Some medicines that may be harmful to your baby can pass through breast milk.

e Itis possible to become pregnant while breastfeeding. If birth control is desired, ask your health care provider about options that will be safe while breastfeeding your baby.

Where to find more information: La Leche League International: www.llli.org Contact a health care provider if:

e You feel like you want to stop breastfeeding or have become frustrated with breastfeeding. e Your nipples are cracked or bleeding. e Your breasts are red, tender, or warm. Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 393




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-887], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-888] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) More Information (continued)

e You have: © Painful breasts or nipples. o A swollen area on either breast. o A fever or chills. o Nausea or vomiting. o Drainage other than breast milk from your nipples. e Your breasts do not become full before feedings by the fifth day after you give birth. You feel sad and depressed. Your baby is: © Too sleepy to eat well. © Having trouble sleeping. © More than | week old and wetting fewer than 6 diapers in a 24-hour period. o Not gaining weight by 5 days of age. e Your baby has fewer than 3 stools in a 24-hour period. e Your baby's skin or the white parts of his or her eyes become yellow.

Get help right away if:

e Your baby is overly tired (/ethargic) and does not want to wake up and feed. e Your baby develops an unexplained fever.

Summary

e Breastfeeding offers many health benefits for infant and mothers.

e Try to breastfeed your infant when he or she shows early signs of hunger.

e Gently tickle or stroke your baby's lips with your finger or nipple to allow the baby to open his or her mouth. Bring the baby to your breast. Make sure that much of the areola is in your baby's mouth. Offer one side and burp the baby before you offer the other side.

e Talk with your health care provider or lactation consultant if you have questions or you face problems as you breastfeed.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-891], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-892] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) More Information (continued)

Breast Pumping Tips

Breast pumping is a way to get milk out of your breasts. You will then store the milk for your baby to use when you are away from home. There are three ways to pump. You can:

e Use your hand to massage and squeeze your breast (hand expression). e Use a hand-held machine to manually pump your milk. e Use an electric machine to pump your milk.

In the beginning you may not get much milk. After a few days your breasts should make more. Pumping can help you start making milk after your baby is born. Pumping helps you to keep making milk when you are away from your baby.

When should I pump?

You can start pumping soon after your baby is born. Follow these tips:

e When you are with your baby: o Pump after you breastfeed. o Pump from the free breast while you breastfeed. e When you are away from your baby: o Pump every 2-3 hours for 15 minutes. o Pump both breasts at the same time if you can. If your baby drinks formula, pump around the time your baby gets the formula. If you drank alcohol, wait 2 hours before you pump. e Ifyou are going to have surgery, ask your doctor when you should pump again.

How do I get ready to pump?

Take steps to relax. Try these things to help your milk come in:

Smell your baby's blanket or clothes.

Look at a picture or video of your baby.

Sit in a quiet, private space.

Massage your breast and nipple.

Place a cloth on your breast. The cloth should be warm and a little wet. Play relaxing music.

Picture your milk flowing.

What are some tips?

General tips for pumping breast milk

e Always wash your hands before pumping. a _ = e Ifyou do not get much milk or if pumping hurts, try different pump settings or a > ~ 8 different kind of pump.

Drink enough fluid so your pee (urine) is clear or pale yellow.

Wear clothing that opens in the front or is easy to take off.

Pump milk into a clean bottle or container.

Do not use anything that has nicotine or tobacco. Examples are cigarettes and e-cigarettes. If you need help quitting, ask your doctor.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 395




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-893], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-894] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) More Information (continued)

Tips for storing breast milk

e Store breast milk in a clean, BPA-free container. These include: © A glass or plastic bottle. o A milk storage bag. e Store only 24 ounces of breast milk in each container. e Swirl the breast milk in the container. Do not shake it. e Write down the date you pumped the milk on the container. e This is how long you can store breast milk: © Room temperature: 6-8 hours. It is best to use the milk within 4 hours. © Cooler with ice packs: 24 hours. © Refrigerator: 5—8 days, if the milk is clean. It is best to use the milk within 3 days. o Freezer: 9-12 months, if the milk is clean and stored away from the freezer door. It is best to use the milk within 6 months. e Put milk in the back of the refrigerator or freezer. e Thaw frozen milk using warm water. Do not use the microwave.

Tips for choosing a breast pump

When choosing a pump, keep the following things in mind:

Manual breast pumps do not need electricity. They cost less. They can be hard to use.

Electric breast pumps use electricity. They are more expensive. They are easier to use. They collect more milk. The suction cup (flange) should be the right size.

Before you buy the pump, check if your insurance will pay for it.

Tips for caring for a breast pump

e Check the manual that came with your pump for cleaning tips. e Clean the pump after you use it. To do this: o Wipe down the electrical part. Use a dry cloth or paper towel. Do not put this part in water or in cleaning products. o Wash the plastic parts with soap and warm water. Or use the dishwasher if the manual says it is safe. You do not need to clean the tubing unless it touched breast milk. o Let all the parts air dry. Avoid drying them with a cloth or towel. o When the parts are clean and dry, put the pump back together. Then store the pump. e If there is water in the tubing when you want to pump: 1. Attach the tubing to the pump. 2. Turn on the pump. 3. Turn off the pump when the tube is dry. e Try not to touch the inside of pump parts.

Summary

e Pumping can help you start making milk after your baby is born. It lets you keep making milk when you are away from your baby.

e When you are away from your baby, pump for about 15 minutes every 2—3 hours. Pump both breasts at the same time, if you can.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 396

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-899], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-900] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) More Information (continued)

Eating Plan for Breastfeeding Women

On the days when a woman is breastfeeding, her body burns more calories than usual (about 400-500 extra calories). It is important that you eat enough calories by consuming a variety of healthy foods. You do not need to follow a special diet while breastfeeding your baby. Focus on making healthy choices to help support and maintain your milk production.

What are tips for following this plan? Medicines, vitamins, and supplements

e Talk with your health care provider about all medicines that you are taking. Medicines can pass to your baby through your breast milk. Certain medicines may slow, delay, or lessen your milk production and may be harmful to your baby.

e Continue to take your prenatal vitamins and any other supplements as told by your health care provider.

e If you follow a special diet (such as a vegan diet) or you have certain health conditions, you may need additional supplements. Talk with your health care provider about this.

Amount and variety

e Consume enough calories each day to maintain your milk supply.

e Drink plenty of water to help maintain your milk supply and stay nourished yourself. Aim for 64 oz (2 L) or more a day, or as much as told by your health care provider. Your urine should be pale yellow in color. If you notice that your urine is dark yellow, drink more water.

¢ Continue to follow a healthy, well-balanced diet that includes healthy snacks. The taste of your milk will be affected by what you eat. Eating different foods will expose your baby to different tastes, which may help your baby to accept solid foods more easily later on.

What to limit or avoid

e Limit your overall intake of foods that have "empty calories." These are foods that have little nutritional value, such as sweets, desserts, candies, and sugar-sweetened beverages.

e Avoid drinking more than 16—24 oz (473-710 mL) of caffeinated drinks in a day. Caffeine is dehydrating. It will also enter your breast milk, and this might bother your baby or interfere with his or her sleep.

e Avoid drinking more than one alcoholic drink per day. Examples of one drink are a 5-oz glass of wine, a 12-0z beer, or 1.5 fluid oz of 80 proof distilled spirits (40% alcohol). It is better for your baby if:

o You wait to have your first alcoholic drink until your breastfeeding has been well established, which is usually after 2—3 months.

o You wait 4 hours or longer to breastfeed after you have an alcoholic drink. As an alternative, you may pump your breast milk before you drink alcohol. You can store that milk to feed to your baby at a later time.

e You may notice that certain foods cause you or your baby to have increased gas and may cause fussiness in your baby. If you notice increased gas or fussiness in your baby when you eat these foods, consider avoiding them while breastfeeding.

Food safety

e To prevent food-borne illnesses, practice good food safety and cleanliness, such as washing your hands before you eat and after you prepare raw meat.

What foods can I eat? Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 398




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-901], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-902] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued

Fruits

All fruits. Try to eat a variety of colors and types of fruits to get a full range of vitamins and minerals. Remember to wash your fruits well before eating.

Vegetables

All vegetables. Try to eat a variety of colors and types of vegetables to get a full range of vitamins and minerals. Remember to wash your vegetables well before eating.

Grains All grains. Try to choose whole grains, such as whole-wheat bread, oatmeal, or brown rice. Meats and other protein foods

Lean meats. Try to eat chicken, turkey, fish, and lean cuts of beef, veal, or pork. If you eat fish or seafood, choose options that are low in mercury, such as salmon, canned light tuna, catfish, shrimp, crab, and lobster. Other good protein sources include tofu, tempeh, beans, eggs, peanut butter, and other nut butters.

Dairy

Dairy is okay to eat. Continue to drink milk and milk alternatives (such as almond milk), or eat yogurt, cheese, cottage cheese, or sour cream.

Beverages

Most beverages are okay.

Fats and oils

All fats and oils are okay. Sweets and desserts

All sweets and desserts are okay. Seasoning and other foods

All seasonings are okay.

The items listed above may not be a complete list of foods and beverages you can eat. Contact a dietitian for more information.

What foods are not recommended? Meats and other protein foods

Avoid eating fish that have high mercury content, such as tilefish, shark, swordfish, and king mackerel. To learn more about mercury in fish, talk with your health care provider or look for online resources, such as:

e www.fda.gov

Beverages

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 399




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-903], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-904] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) More Information (continued)

Avoid drinking sugar-sweetened beverages such as sodas, teas, or energy drinks. Limit the amount of caffeine and alcohol that you drink.

The items listed above may not be a complete list of foods and beverages you should avoid. Contact a dietitian for more information.

Where to find more information

You can talk with your health care provider or use an online calorie calculator to determine how many calories you need to eat each day while breastfeeding, such as:

¢ www.choosemyplate.gov

Summary

e Focus on making healthy choices to help support and maintain your milk production. To maintain a good milk supply and stay nourished yourself, it is also important that you drink plenty of water.

e Talk with your health care provider about all medicines that you are taking. Continue to take your prenatal vitamins and any other supplements as told by your health care provider.

e Avoid drinking large amounts of caffeine or alcohol.

e Continue to follow a healthy, well-balanced diet that includes healthy snacks. The taste of your milk will be affected by what you eat. Eating different foods will expose your baby to different tastes, which may help your baby to accept solid foods more easily later on.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

[DOB-908] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued)

Storing Breast Milk

Breast milk is a living fluid that contains infection-fighting cells (antibodies). Pre-pumped (expressed) breast milk needs to be stored in a certain way so that it remains effective in protecting your baby against infections. The following guidelines are for storing breast milk for a healthy, full-term infant.

How long can breast milk be stored?

Milk can be stored for up to 4 hours at room temperature, or 60—85°F (15.6—-19.4°C). However, it is acceptable to allow the milk to sit for 6-8 hours if the pump parts and containers are well cleaned.

Milk can be stored for 3 days in a refrigerator at less than 39°F (3.9°C). However, it is acceptable to allow the milk to sit for up to 8 days if the pump parts and containers are well cleaned.

Milk can be stored for 2 weeks in a freezer compartment inside a refrigerator.

Milk can be stored for 3-6 months in a freezer unit with a separate door.

Milk can be stored for 6—12 months in a deep freezer at -4°F (-20°C). A deep freezer is a chest or stand-alone freezer that is not opened very often and is kept at a colder temperature than a regular freezer.

How should I store breast milk?

Milk may be stored in:

o A glass container.

o A hard, BPA-free plastic container.

o A plastic bag specially designed for storing breast milk. Many women like these because they take up less space than other containers and can be attached directly to the breast pump.

Store your milk in 24 oz. (60-120 mL) servings. This makes it easier to thaw the milk. It also helps you avoid having to throw out milk that your baby does not drink.

Leave an inch or so at the top of the bag or bottle so the milk has room to expand as it freezes.

Label each container with the date and time the milk was pumped so that you use the milk in the order that it was pumped.

If you will be freezing the milk, freeze it within 24 hours of pumping. Store it in the back of the freezer to prevent the milk from being affected by temperature changes when the freezer door is opened.

How do I thaw frozen breast milk?

Frozen milk can be thawed:

o Ina refrigerator.

o Under warm, running tap water.

o Ina pan of warm water that has been heated on the stove. Do not heat milk directly on the stove or in the microwave. The milk will heat unevenly, and it may burn the baby. It will also destroy some of the milk’s infection-fighting properties. Thawed milk can be stored in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours, but it should not be refrozen. If you want to freeze freshly pumped milk along with milk that is already frozen, take these steps to prevent the fresh milk from thawing out the frozen milk:

o Chill the fresh milk in the refrigerator for 30 minutes before adding it to the frozen milk.

o Make sure that the amount of fresh milk you add is smaller than the amount of frozen milk.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss

More Information (continued

Preventing Secondhand Smoke Exposure During Pregnancy and Breastfeeding

Secondhand smoke is the smoke that comes from burning cigarettes, pipes, or cigars. Secondhand smoke also comes from air exhaled by people who are smoking. Smoking also leaves dangerous chemicals on walls, carpets, furniture, clothing, and inside cars (thirdhandsmoke).

Even if you do not smoke, secondhand or thirdhand smoke can affect you and your baby before and after birth.

What actions can I take?

¢ Do not smoke. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider for help. e Do not let anyone smoke in your house or car. e¢ Do not work with babysitters or caregivers who smoke. They may bring secondhand and thirdhand smoke near your baby. e Avoid places where people smoke. If you are exposed to secondhand or thirdhand smoke at work, talk with your employer about how to avoid this. @ e Ifyou are exposed to secondhand smoke, change your clothes and wash your hands before breastfeeding.

Why are these changes important?

Secondhand smoke contains many chemicals that are known to cause cancer. Cigarettes also contain a stimulant drug (nicotine) that can affect your baby's development. When you breathe in smoke, the harmful substances that you inhale can pass to your baby through the placenta or through breast milk and cause serious or life-threatening health problems that affect you and your baby.

What can happen if changes are not made?

Breathing in secondhand smoke during pregnancy:

e Decreases blood flow and oxygen to your baby.

e Increases your baby's risk for birth defects, including heart defects.

e Increases your baby's heart rate.

e Slows your baby's growth in the uterus (intrauterine growth retardation).

Babies born to women who were exposed to secondhand smoke during pregnancy may:

Have unpleasant symptoms caused by stopping the nicotine exposure (withdrawal). Need to stay in the hospital for special care. Be born too small. Develop ear or upper respiratory infections. Have a high risk of: o Serious health problems, including lung and breathing problems. o Long-term (chronic) or lifelong disabilities such as cerebral palsy, mental retardation, and learning problems. Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS). Becoming obese. Developing behavior problems.

Oo OO

Where to find more information

You can find more information about preventing secondhand smoke exposure from:

e U.S. Department of Health and Human Services: smokefree.gov e Centers for Disease Control and Prevention: cdc.gov e American Pregnancy Association: americanpregnancy.org

Summary

e Secondhand and thirdhand smoke can cause serious health problems for babies before and after birth.

e Not smoking and keeping your home and car smoke-free are the best ways to protect your baby before and after birth.

Ibuprofen tablets and capsules

What is this medicine?

IBUPROFEN (eye BYOO proe fen) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It is used for dental pain, fever, headaches or migraines, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or painful monthly periods. It can also relieve minor aches and pains caused by a cold, flu, or sore throat.

This medicine may be used for other purposes; ask your health care provider or pharmacist if you have questions.

COMMON BRAND NAME(S): Advil, Advil Junior Strength, Advil Migraine, Genpril, Ibren, IBU, Ibupak, Midol, Midol Cramps and Body Aches, Motrin, Motrin IB, Motrin Junior Strength, Motrin Migraine Pain, Samson-8, Toxicology Saliva Collection

What should I tell my health care provider before I take this medicine? They need to know if you have any of these conditions:

cigarette smoker

coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery within the past 2 weeks drink more than 3 alcohol-containing drinks a day

heart disease

high blood pressure

history of stomach bleeding

kidney disease

liver disease

lung or breathing disease, like asthma

an unusual or allergic reaction to ibuprofen, aspirin, other NSAIDs, other medicines, foods, dyes, or preservatives

pregnant or trying to get pregnant

e breast-feeding

How should I use this medicine?

Take this medicine by mouth with a glass of water. Follow the directions on the prescription label. Take this medicine with food if your stomach gets upset. Try to not lie down for at least 10 minutes after you take the medicine. Take your medicine at regular intervals. Do not take your medicine more often than directed.

A special MedGuide will be given to you by the pharmacist with each prescription and refill. Be sure to read this information carefully each time.

Talk to your pediatrician regarding the use of this medicine in children. Special care may be needed.

Overdosage: If you think you have taken too much of this medicine contact a poison control center or emergency room at once.

NOTE: This medicine is only for you. Do not share this medicine with others. What if I miss a dose?

If you miss a dose, take it as soon as you can. If it is almost time for your next dose, take only that dose. Do not take double or extra doses.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 405




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-921], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-922] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) More Information (continued)

What may interact with this medicine? Do not take this medicine with any of the following medications:

e cidofovir

e ketorolac

e methotrexate e pemetrexed

This medicine may also interact with the following medications:

e alcohol

e aspirin

e diuretics

e lithium

e other drugs for inflammation like prednisone

e warfarin

This list may not describe all possible interactions. Give your health care provider a list of all the medicines, herbs, non-prescription drugs, or dietary supplements you use. Also tell them if you smoke, drink alcohol, or use illegal drugs. Some items may interact with your medicine.

What should I watch for while using this medicine? Tell your doctor or healthcare provider if your symptoms do not start to get better or if they get worse.

This medicine may cause serious skin reactions. They can happen weeks to months after starting the medicine. Contact your healthcare provider right away if you notice fevers or flu-like symptoms with a rash. The rash may be red or purple and then turn into blisters or peeling of the skin. Or, you might notice a red rash with swelling of the face, lips or lymph nodes in your neck or under your arms.

This medicine does not prevent heart attack or stroke. In fact, this medicine may increase the chance of a heart attack or stroke. The chance may increase with longer use of this medicine and in people who have heart disease. If you take aspirin to prevent heart attack or stroke, talk with your doctor or healthcare provider.

Do not take other medicines that contain aspirin, ibuprofen, or naproxen with this medicine. Side effects such as stomach upset, nausea, or ulcers may be more likely to occur. Many medicines available without a prescription should not be taken with this medicine.

This medicine can cause ulcers and bleeding in the stomach and intestines at any time during treatment. Ulcers and bleeding can happen without warning symptoms and can cause death. To reduce your risk, do not smoke cigarettes or drink alcohol while you are taking this medicine.

You may get drowsy or dizzy. Do not drive, use machinery, or do anything that needs mental alertness until you know how this medicine affects you. Do not stand or sit up quickly, especially if you are an older patient. This reduces the risk of dizzy or fainting spells.

This medicine can cause you to bleed more easily. Try to avoid damage to your teeth and gums when you brush or floss your teeth.

This medicine may be used to treat migraines. If you take migraine medicines for 10 or more days a month, your migraines may get worse. Keep a diary of headache days and medicine use. Contact your healthcare provider if your

migraine attacks occur more frequently. What side effects may I notice from receiving this medicine? Side effects that you should report to your doctor or health care professional as soon as possible:

allergic reactions like skin rash, itching or hives, swelling of the face, lips, or tongue

redness, blistering, peeling or loosening of the skin, including inside the mouth

severe stomach pain

signs and symptoms of bleeding such as bloody or black, tarry stools; red or dark-brown urine; spitting up blood

or brown material that looks like coffee grounds; red spots on the skin; unusual bruising or bleeding from the

eye, gums, or nose

e signs and symptoms of a blood clot such as changes in vision; chest pain; severe, sudden headache; trouble speaking; sudden numbness or weakness of the face, arm, or leg

e unexplained weight gain or swelling

e unusually weak or tired

e yellowing of eyes or skin

Side effects that usually do not require medical attention (report to your doctor or health care professional if they continue or are bothersome):

bruising

diarrhea

dizziness, drowsiness headache

nausea, vomiting

This list may not describe all possible side effects. Call your doctor for medical advice about side effects. You may report side effects to FDA at 1-800-FDA-1088.

Where should I keep my medicine? Keep out of the reach of children.

Store at room temperature between 15 and 30 degrees C (59 and 86 degrees F). Keep container tightly closed. Throw away any unused medicine after the expiration date.

NOTE: This sheet is a summary. It may not cover all possible information. If you have questions about this medicine, talk to your doctor, pharmacist, or health care provider.

Postpartum Care After Cesarean Delivery

This sheet gives you information about how to care for yourself from the time you deliver your baby to up to 6-12 weeks after delivery (postpartum period). Your health care provider may also give you more specific instructions. If you have problems or questions, contact your health care provider.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. e If you were prescribed an antibiotic medicine, take it as told by your health care provider. Do not stop taking the antibiotic even if you start to feel better. e Ask your health care provider if the medicine prescribed to you: o Requires you to avoid driving or using heavy machinery. © Can cause constipation. You may need to take actions to prevent or treat constipation, such as: * Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. " Take over-the-counter or prescription medicines. " Eat foods that are high in fiber, such as beans, whole grains, and fresh fruits and vegetables. * Limit foods that are high in fat and processed sugars, such as fried or sweet foods.

Activity

e Gradually return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider.

e Avoid activities that take a lot of effort and energy (are strenuous) until approved by your health care provider. Walking at a slow to moderate pace is usually safe. Ask your health care provider what activities are safe for you.

°o Do not lift anything that is heavier than your baby or 10 lb (4.5 kg) as told by your health care provider. © Do not vacuum, climb stairs, or drive a car for as long as told by your health care provider.

e If possible, have someone help you at home until you are able to do your usual activities yourself.

e Rest as much as possible. Try to rest or take naps while your baby is sleeping.

Vaginal bleeding

e It is normal to have vaginal bleeding (/ochia) after delivery. Wear a sanitary pad to absorb vaginal bleeding and discharge. © During the first week after delivery, the amount and appearance of lochia is often similar to a menstrual period. © Over the next few weeks, it will gradually decrease to a dry, yellow-brown discharge. o For most women, lochia stops completely by 4-6 weeks after delivery. Vaginal bleeding can vary from woman to woman. e Change your sanitary pads frequently. Watch for any changes in your flow, such as: o A sudden increase in volume. o A change in color. © Large blood clots. e Ifyou pass a blood clot, save it and call your health care provider to discuss. Do not flush blood clots down the toilet before you get instructions from your health care provider. e Do not use tampons or douches until your health care provider says this is safe. e Ifyou are not breastfeeding, your period should return 6-8 weeks after delivery. If you are breastfeeding, your period may return anytime between 8 weeks after delivery and the time that you stop breastfeeding.

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-927], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-928] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued

e If your C-section (Cesarean section) was unplanned, and you were allowed to labor and push before delivery, you may have pain, swelling, and discomfort of the tissue between your vaginal opening and your anus (perineum). You may also have an incision in the tissue (episiotomy) or the tissue may have torn during delivery. Follow these instructions as told by your health care provider:

© Keep your perineum clean and dry as told by your health care provider. Use medicated pads and pain- relieving sprays and creams as directed.

o If you have an episiotomy or vaginal tear, check the area every day for signs of infection. Check for:

" Redness, swelling, or pain. * Fluid or blood.

* Warmth.

* Pus or a bad smell.

o You may be given a squirt bottle to use instead of wiping to clean the perineum area after you go to the bathroom. As you start healing, you may use the squirt bottle before wiping yourself. Make sure to wipe gently.

o To relieve pain caused by an episiotomy, vaginal tear, or hemorrhoids, try taking a warm sitz bath 2-3 times a day. A sitz bath is a warm water bath that is taken while you are sitting down. The water should only come up to your hips and should cover your buttocks.

Perineal care

Breast care

e Within the first few days after delivery, your breasts may feel heavy, full, and uncomfortable (breast engorgement). You may also have milk leaking from your breasts. Your health care provider can suggest ways to help relieve breast discomfort. Breast engorgement should go away within a few days.

e Ifyou are breastfeeding:

o Wear a bra that supports your breasts and fits you well.

© Keep your nipples clean and dry. Apply creams and ointments as told by your health care provider.

o You may need to use breast pads to absorb milk leakage.

o You may have uterine contractions every time you breastfeed for several weeks after delivery. Uterine contractions help your uterus return to its normal size.

o If you have any problems with breastfeeding, work with your health care provider or a lactation consultant.

e Ifyou are not breastfeeding:

o Avoid touching your breasts as this can make your breasts produce more milk. o Wear a well-fitting bra and use cold packs to help with swelling. °o Do not squeeze out (express) milk. This causes you to make more milk.

Intimacy and sexuality

e Ask your health care provider when you can engage in sexual activity. This may depend on your: © Risk of infection. o Healing rate. © Comfort and desire to engage in sexual activity. e You are able to get pregnant after delivery, even if you have not had your period. If desired, talk with your health care provider about methods of family planning or birth control (contraception).

Lifestyle

e Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes, e-cigarettes, and chewing tobacco. If Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 409




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-929], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-930] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued

you need help quitting, ask your health care provider. e Do not drink alcohol, especially if you are breastfeeding.

Eating and drinking e Drink enough fluid to keep your urine pale yellow. CAEL CAT e Eat high-fiber foods every day. These may help prevent or relieve constipation. pie High-fiber foods include: or i | yellow

o Whole grain cereals and breads. o Brown rice. o Beans. © Fresh fruits and vegetables. e Take your prenatal vitamins until your postpartum checkup or until your health care provider tells you it is okay to stop.

General instructions

¢ Keep all follow-up visits for you and your baby as told by your health care provider. Most women visit their health care provider for a postpartum checkup within the first 3-6 weeks after delivery.

Contact a health care provider if you:

Feel unable to cope with the changes that a new baby brings to your life, and these feelings do not go away. Feel unusually sad or worried.

Have breasts that are painful, hard, or turn red.

Have a fever.

Have trouble holding urine or keeping urine from leaking.

Have little or no interest in activities you used to enjoy.

Have not breastfed at all and you have not had a menstrual period for 12 weeks after delivery.

Have stopped breastfeeding and you have not had a menstrual period for 12 weeks after you stopped breastfeeding.

Have questions about caring for yourself or your baby.

e Pass a blood clot from your vagina.

Get help right away if you:

Have chest pain.

Have difficulty breathing.

Have sudden, severe leg pain.

Have severe pain or cramping in your abdomen. Bleed from your vagina so much that you fill more than one sanitary pad in one hour. Bleeding should not be heavier than your heaviest period.

Develop a severe headache.

Faint.

Have blurred vision or spots in your vision.

Have a bad-smelling vaginal discharge.

Have thoughts about hurting yourself or your baby.

If you ever feel like you may hurt yourself or others, or have thoughts about taking your own life, get help right away. You can go to your nearest emergency department or call:

e A suicide crisis helpline, such as the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-[PHONE-933]. This is open 24 hours a day.

Summary

e The period of time from when you deliver your baby to up to 6-12 weeks after delivery is called the postpartum period.

e Gradually return to your normal activities as told by your health care provider.

¢ Keep all follow-up visits for you and your baby as told by your health care provider.

Postpartum Baby Blues

The postpartum period begins right after the birth of a baby. During this time, there is often a lot of joy and excitement. It is also a time of many changes in the life of the parents. No matter how many times a mother gives birth, each child brings new challenges to the family, including different ways of relating to one another.

It is common to have feelings of excitement along with confusing changes in moods, emotions, and thoughts. You may feel happy one minute and sad or stressed the next. These feelings of sadness usually happen in the period right after you have your baby, and they go away within a week or two. This is called the "baby blues."

What are the causes?

There is no known cause of baby blues. It is likely caused by a combination of factors. However, changes in hormone levels after childbirth are believed to trigger some of the symptoms.

Other factors that can play a role in these mood changes include:

e Lack of sleep. e Stressful life events, such as poverty, caring for a loved one, or death of a loved one. e Genetics.

What are the signs or symptoms?

Symptoms of this condition include:

Brief changes in mood, such as going from extreme happiness to sadness. Decreased concentration.

Difficulty sleeping.

Crying spells and tearfulness.

Loss of appetite.

Irritability.

Anxiety.

If the symptoms of baby blues last for more than 2 weeks or become more severe, you may have postpartum depression.

How is this diagnosed?

This condition is diagnosed based on an evaluation of your symptoms. There are no medical or lab tests that lead to a diagnosis, but there are various questionnaires that a health care provider may use to identify women with the baby blues or postpartum depression.

How is this treated?

Treatment is not needed for this condition. The baby blues usually go away on their own in 1—2 weeks. Social support is often all that is needed. You will be encouraged to get adequate sleep and rest.

Follow these instructions at home:

Lifestyle

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-938], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-939] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)

More Information (continued

Get as much rest as you can. Take a nap when the baby sleeps.

e Exercise regularly as told by your health care provider. Some women find yoga and walking to be helpful.

e Eat a balanced and nourishing diet. This includes plenty of fruits and vegetables, whole grains, and lean proteins.

e Do little things that you enjoy. Have a cup of tea, take a bubble bath, read your favorite magazine, or listen to your favorite music.

e Avoid alcohol.

e Ask for help with household chores, cooking, grocery shopping, or running errands. Do not try to do everything yourself. Consider hiring a postpartum doula to help. This is a professional who specializes in providing support to new mothers.

e Try not to make any major life changes during pregnancy or right after giving birth. This can add stress.

General instructions

e Talk to people close to you about how you are feeling. Get support from your partner, family members, friends, or other new moms. You may want to join a support group. e Find ways to cope with stress. This may include: o Writing your thoughts and feelings in a journal. o Spending time outside. o Spending time with people who make you laugh. Try to stay positive in how you think. Think about the things you are grateful for. Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your health care provider. Let your health care provider know if you have any concerns. Keep all postpartum visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Contact a health care provider if: e Your baby blues do not go away after 2 weeks. Get help right away if:

e You have thoughts of taking your own life (suicidal thoughts). e You think you may harm the baby or other people. e You see or hear things that are not there (hallucinations).

Summary

e After giving birth, you may feel happy one minute and sad or stressed the next. Feelings of sadness that happen right after the baby is born and go away after a week or two are called the "baby blues."

e You can manage the baby blues by getting enough rest, eating a healthy diet, exercising, spending time with supportive people, and finding ways to cope with stress.

e If feelings of sadness and stress last longer than 2 weeks or get in the way of caring for your baby, talk to your health care provider. This may mean you have postpartum depression.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-942], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021

[DOB-943] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued) PREVENTING SURGICAL SITE INFECTIONS

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call 911 e Call a suicide hotline to speak to a counselor. 1-800-273-TALK or 1-800-SUICIDE

Screenings - 02/03/21 0810 Baptist Health High Risk Falls Assessment (If Fall score is >/=13, add the Fall Risk CPG to the care plan)

Fallen in past 6 months 5--> Yes

CONSENT TO DIAGNOSTIC TESTS, PROCEDURES, & MEDICALTREATMENT: | voluntarily consent to (and consent on behalf of any child born to me while hospitalized) care involving routine diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment as ordered by my admitting and treating physicians, including their assistants or designees. | consent to receive medical care through the use of telehealth and/or remote patient monitoring. | further consent to the interpretation of diagnostic studies from an off-site location using telehealth technologies. | consent to medical, nursing, allied health and other students observing and participating in my care under the supervision of a qualified professional. | consent to photographic recordings or reproducible images during the surgical, medical, and/or diagnostic procedure(s) and their use for identification, scientific, educational, or research purposes. | also consent to testing for communicable and blood- borne infectious diseases, such as hepatitis, tuberculosis, and the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), if a provider orders testing for diagnostic purposes or if there has been an exposure to healthcare personnel. | have been given no guarantees about the results that may be obtained from my care. | understand and agree that the results of diagnostic tests, procedures and medical treatment ordered and provided by providers while | am hospitalized, including a notice of admission to the hospital will be provided to my primary care physician on record, if any.

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL: Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. In most situations, advanced practitioners, including but not limited to, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, surgical assistants and psychologists, providing treatment to patients in the Hospital are not employed by the Hospital. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers, or the Hospital bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by the providers.

3. FINANCIAL AGREEMENT AND ASSIGNMENT OF BENEFITS: | agree that | am responsible for payment of my Hospital bill. Payment of any portion of my bill not covered by a third party payor is due upon discharge from the Hospital unless the Hospital has agreed to other arrangements. | agree to the assignment of all third party payor benefits to the Hospital and to any health care provider rendering services to me. | acknowledge and agree that Hospital is not required to accept assignment of any third party payor benefits, in which case, | may receive a bill from Hospital for the full amount of charges related to any care or treatment provided to me or my guarantee by Hospital and | agree to pay the Hospital and other health care providers for such charges. Moreover, | understand that Hospital may accept payment from third party payors with whom it does not have a written signed agreement and that such acceptance of payment does not constitute acceptance by Hospital of any reimbursement rates established by such third party payors and that | may receive a bill from Hospital for the difference between the rate paid by such third party payors and the Hospital’s charges. | agree to pay the Hospital and other health care providers for all charges for services that are not covered or paid by any third party payor regardless of the reason, including but not limited to a determination by any third party payor that such services are not covered services or medically necessary. To the extent | am a Medicare or Medicaid beneficiary, | certify that any information | provide in applying for payment under Title XVIII (Medicare) or Title XIX (Medicaid) of the Social Security Act is correct. | request payment of authorized benefits to be made on my behalf to Baptist Health facility or to any health care provider rendering services to me by the Medicare or Medicaid program. | hereby irrevocably appoint Hospital as my authorized representative to pursue any claims, penalties, and administrative and/or legal remedies on my behalf for collection against any responsible payer, employer-sponsored medical benefit plans, third party liability carrier or, any other responsible third party for any and all benefits due me for the payment of charges associated with my treatment. This assignment shall not be construed as an obligation of the Hospital to pursue any such right of recovery. | agree to take all actions necessary to assist Hospital in collecting payment from any such third party payer. | understand that after reasonable notice, any unpaid account may be turned over to a collection agency and/or attorney for collection. Should it be necessary for the Hospital to pursue collection, | agree to pay all reasonable collection costs, including court costs and attorney’s fees incurred by the Hospital in collecting my account. Pursuant to the Fair Credit Reporting Act (15 USC §1681b(a)(2)), | authorize a credit reporting agency engaged by Hospital or its collection agents to release to the Hospital or any of its representatives or affiliates, my consumer report. | understand that the purpose of this authorization and request is to obtain my consumer report, which may be used to determine the availability of or the need for financial assistance, charity care, or insurance coverage for me and may be used for billing and collection purposes related to payment for services provided to me. | understand this authorization is valid until my Hospital account for all services is closed.

4. OUTPATIENT MEDICARE PATIENTS: Medicare does not cover prescription drugs with only a few exceptions. |

[DOB-1109] - LABOR INDUCTION in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR AND DELIVERY (continued) Instructions for After Discharge

Patient Instructions None

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www-.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call 911 e Call a suicide hotline to speak to a counselor. 1-800-273-TALK or 1-800-SUICIDE

OBED Note OBHG

Patient Name: Anella Noble

Date of Birth: 11/7/2000

MRN: [PHONE-1141]

Admission Date: 1/17/2021 12:18 PM Date of Service: 1/17/2021

Chief Complaint: Fall (patient presents to labor and delivery at 39 weeks gestation with complaints of a fall at 1145 today)

Subjective

Anella Noble is a 20 y.o. female G1P0 at 39w2d with Estimated Date of Delivery: 1/22/21 who presents with the chief complaint listed above. She sees Lewis, Lauren S, MD for her prenatal care. Her pregnancy has been complicated by: uncomplicated. Patient reports tripping on laundry basket at home and falling on outstretched hands. She does report bottom of abdomen hitting floor slightly. Upon arrival she denies being in any pain. She denies bleeding, loss of fluid, or decreased fetal movement. She is not scheduled for induction yet.

She describes fetal movement as normal. She denies rupture of membranes. She denies vaginal bleeding. She is not feeling contractions.

Objective There are no active problems to display for this patient.

[DOB-1149] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued)

ED Notes (continued)

Assessment/Plan

ASSESSMENT/PLAN:

Anella Noble is a 20 y.o. female G1P0 at 39w2d who presented with: fall at home. Patient in no discomfort on admission and denying contractions, bleeding, and loss of fluid. Cervix checked and closed. Abruption labs sent and WNL. Abdominal US reassuring. Patient monitored for four hours after fall. At this point, fetal heart rate continued to be category 1 but tocometer did show contractions with irritability although the patient denied feeling this. As she was continuing to have uterine activity, patient admitted for 24 hour observation. No current evidence of active abruption. Plan of care discussed with Dr. Johnson who will assume care as inpatient.

Final Impression:

e Pregnancy at 39w2d

e Reactive NST. CAT 1 tracing

e Fall in pregnancy

e Maternal vital signs were reviewed and were unremarkable

Author: Johnson, Michele D, MD Service: Obstetrics Author Type: Physician Filed: 01/17/21 1902 Date of Service: 01/17/21 1855 Creation Time: 01/17/21 1855 Status: Signed Editor: Johnson, Michele D, MD (Physician)

BH Louisville Obstetric History and Physical

Patient Name: Anella Noble DOB: 11/7/2000 MRN: [PHONE-1155]

Chief Complaint Patient presents with * Fall

patient presents to labor and delivery at 39 weeks gestation with complaints of a fall at 1145 today

Subjective

Patient is a 20 y.o. female G1P0 currently at 39w2d, who presents after a fall this am. Tripped over a laundry basket and fell forward - stopped her fall with her hands but her abdomen may have hit slightly.

Reports good FM. No VB/LOF. Frequent contractions noted but reports not feeling them.

Her prenatal care has been unremarkable.

Past Medical History: History reviewed. No pertinent past medical history. Past Surgical History History reviewed. No pertinent surgical history. Family History: History reviewed. No pertinent family history.

Social History: reports that she has never smoked. She has never used smokeless tobacco. reports no history of alcohol use. reports no history of drug use.

Allergies: Patient has no known allergies.

Past OB History:

OB History Gravida Para Term Preterm AB Living 1 0 0 0 0 0
