SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1282], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1283] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued) Care Plan (continued)

Goal: Patient-Specific Goal (Individualized)

Dates: Start: 01/17/21 Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Goal: Absence of Hospital-Acquired Illness or Injury

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Intervention: Identify and Manage Fall Risk Frequency: Per CPG Description: Perform standard risk assessment with a validated tool or comprehensive approach

appropriate to the patient on admission; reassess fall risk frequently, with change in status or transfer to another level of care.

Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team.

Determine need for increased observation, equipment and environmental modification, such as low bed and signage, as well as supportive, nonskid footwear.

Adjust safety measures to individual developmental age, stage and identified risk factors. Reinforce the importance of safety and physical activity with patient and family.

Perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment, personal needs, including assistance with toileting.

Intervention: Prevent Skin Injury

Frequency: Per CPG Description: Assess skin risk on admission and at regular intervals throughout hospital stay. Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry. Maintain adequate skin hydration. Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based on patient-specific risk factors. Match turning and repositioning schedule to clinical condition. Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition if unable to complete independently. Float heels off bed. Avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon. Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices. Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.

Intervention: Prevent and Manage VTE (venous thromboembolism) Risk

Frequency: Per CPG Description: Assess for VTE risk. Encourage/assist with early ambulation. Initiate and maintain compression or other therapy, as indicated based on identified risk in accordance with organizational protocol/provider order. Encourage both active and passive leg exercises while in bed, if unable to ambulate.

Intervention: Prevent Infection

Frequency: Per CPG

Description: Maintain skin and mucous membrane integrity; promote hand, oral and pulmonary hygiene. Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance. Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g., wound, lines, devices). Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated.

Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Intervention: Monitor Pain and Promote Comfort Frequency: Per CPG Description: Assess pain level, treatment efficacy and patient response at regular intervals using a

consistent pain scale.

Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.

Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety measures.

Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 515




Qa TIST HEALTH’ PANY BAP

Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1284], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1285] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued) Care Plan (continued)

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Use a family-focused approach to care.

Develop trust and rapport by proactively providing information, encouraging questions, addressing concerns and offering reassurance.

Acknowledge emotional response to hospitalization.

Recognize and utilize personal coping strategies.

Honor spiritual and cultural preferences.

Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care

Disciplines:

Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Intervention: Mutually Develop Transition Plan

Frequency: Description:

Problem: Perinatal Fall Disciplines:

Per CPG

Identify available resources for support (e.g., family, friends, community).

Identify and address barriers to ongoing treatment and home management (e.g., environmental, financial).

Provide opportunities to practice self-management skills.

Assess and monitor emotional readiness for transition.

Establish/reconnect linkage with outpatient providers or community-based services.

Injury Risk Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Goal: Absence of Fall, Infant Drop and Related Injury

Disciplines:

Nurse, Interdisciplinary

Intervention: Identify and Manage Contributors to Fall Injury Risk

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Reassess fall risk frequently and with change in status or transfer to another level of care. Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team. (e.g., rounds change of shift/provider, patient transport).

Anticipate needs; perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment, personal needs (e.g., toileting) and placement of necessary items.

Promote use of personal vision and auditory aids (e.g., glasses, hearing aids).

Assess assistance level required for safe and effective self-care; provide support as needed (e.g., toileting, mobilization).

Define behavior and activity limits to patient and family to decrease fall or drop risk.

If fall occurs, assess for and treat injury; determine cause; revise fall injury prevention plan. Regularly review medication contribution to fall risk; consider polypharmacy and high-risk medications (e.g., antiepileptic, narcotic, regional block, anesthesia, sedation) given within last 24 hours.

Adjust medication administration times to minimize risk of falling.

Balance adequate pain management with potential for oversedation

Intervention: Promote Injury-Free Environment

Frequency: Description:

Per CPG

Provide a safe, barrier-free environment that encourages independent activity.

Keep care area uncluttered and well-lighted.

Determine need for increased observation or auditory alerts (e.g., bed or chair alarm). Assess equipment and environmental modification needs (e.g., low bed, signage, nonskid footwear, grab bars).

Avoid use of restraints.

Intervention: Prevent Newborn Drop or Fall

Frequency: Description:

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM

Per CPG

Encourage parent/caregiver to use caution if sleepy or sedated when holding and feeding infant.

Facilitate safe transport in bassinet/crib.

Promote cautious handling of wet, active infant during bathing.

Initiate frequent checks during parental holding and feeding, especially at night.

My provider has explained to me that one of the providers in his/her group, a physician's assistant or a first assistant may be involved in this procedure. | understand that these individuals may perform important parts of surgery that are within their scope of practice and for which they have been granted privileges. | consent to the participation of these individuals in my procedure.

INDEPENDENT PRACTITIONERS l understand that many professional services are provided by independent medical doctors and other advanced care practitioners who practice at Baptist Health and that they are not agents or employees of Baptist Health and are considered independent contractors. These independent contractors may include, but are not limited to, anesthesiologists, certified registered nurse anesthetists, pathologists, radiologists, emergency room providers and other medical specialists. ! understand that | will be billed separately for provider services and at rates established by the providers.

RISKS, BENEFITS, & ALTERNATIVES ‘ys

The provider has explained to me: (1) the nature of.th® proposed procedure; (2) the potential benefits, risks, or side effects, including potential problems that might occur during recuperation; (3) the likelihood of achieving goals; (4) reasonable alternatives to the procedure, if any; and (5) the relevant benéfits, risks, and side effects associated with those alternatives, including the possible results of not receiving care, treatment, and sérvices. Based on the provider's explanation of the benefits and risks of this procedure and the alternatives available, ‘agree that the potential benefits of the procedure(s) outweigh the potential risks involved. The provider has explained to n7é that all surgical procedures involve some risk, including risks such as severe loss of blood, infection, cardiac arrest and; in‘tare instances, death. | acknowledge that medicine is not an exact science and that no guarantees have been made to me concerming the] results of the procedure(s) described above. | understand that if complications occur, additional medical treatment may, be required and emergency medical treatment is available.

ADDITIONAL PROCEDURES

| consent to the performance of various procedures in atidition to, or different from, those contemplated, which are related to the above procedure and which the herein above-designated provider and/or his/her assistants or associates may consider necessary or advisable during the course of the procedure.

BLOOD TRANSFUSION AND BLOOD PRODUCTS RISKS, BENEFITS, & ALTERNATIVES

If you wish to not recive blood or blood products please request to sign a refusal form. 1) Some surgical procedures may require blood transfusions or blood products. | was informed that | may need to have blood products administered during the course of my treatment. 2) It has been explained to mejthat the administration of blood products involves the introduction of the product into one of my veins, commonly in the arm using a needle. | understand that the amount of blood products administered is determined by my provider based upon my particular needs. 3) It has been explained to me that the administration of blood products may expose me to certain risks. Minor or temporary reactions, such as slight bruising, swelling, or local infection of the area where the needle pierces the skin is not uncommon. Non-serious reactions may include headache, fever, or a mild skin reaction, such as itching or a rash. Rare, but serious|reactions are possible such as the transmission of diseases including hepatitis and AIDS, which require lengthy treatment and can be fatal. It has been further explained that while procedures for screening blood products for diseases such as AIDS ard hepatitis are effective, these procedures are not perfect and there is a tisk of exposure to AIDS, hepatitis, or other blood-borne disease. | have taken all of these risks into consideration and have decided that the benefits of receiving blood products outweigh the risks. 4)! have been made aware of the reasonably available alternatives to the administration of blood products and the risks and benefits of those alternatives including autologous blood transfusion, directed donation and not having any blood products administered. | understand that I may be able to donate one of several units of my own blood to be used during planned elective surgery and that my provider must determine my ability to donate for myself. This is called autologous transfusion. If | choose to have an autologous transfusion, | understand that the last unit of blood must be donated one week prior to nn use. | also understand that | may choose a directed donation. A

directed donation means | am allowed to select my owr donors. There is no scientific evidence that directed donations are safer but | understand many patients feel more comfortable designating their own donors. The designated donor can donate within thirty (30) days of the anticipated need for the blood. i takes at least seventy-two (72) hours for a unit from a directed donor to be processed and available for transfusion. These arrangements must be made by my provider. Additionally, my procedure may require the services of a periusionist; a perfusionist asgists in the management of the lungs and blood flow during heart surgery.

Provided, however, in the event such is used for scientific and/or educational purposes, my name will not be used and | will not be otherwise identified. | release Baptist Health, its agents, servants, employees, consultants, and providers from any liability in connection with the use of the photographs, pictures, or videotapes.

TISSUE REMOVAL ! understand that this procedure(s) may result in the removal of body tissue or body parts. I consent to their disposal according to the hospital policies and the recommendations of the provider.

VENDOR REPRESENTATIVES / OBSERVERS

! understand that there may be a representative from a company that furnishes equipment to providers and hospitals present in the room during my surgical procedure. | also understand that his/her function is strictly to observe and advise, and that this person has no part in my direct care. | have been informed and understand that, when specifically requested by the provider, a limited and specified number of individuals (excluding parents and blood relatives) are permitted to observe an operation or procedure. | hereby consent to allow those individuals, specifically authorized by the provider, to observe the operation or procedure consented to above.

CLINICAL STUDENTS

Baptist Health participates in clinical education programs with colleges and universities to give students, including but notlimited to, nursing students, medical students, interns, and residents (“students”), experience in clinical practice. Your provider may permit students to observe and participate in your care under the provider's direct supervision.

ADVANCE DIRECTIVE

| acknowledge and agree that any advance directive including, but not limited to, a do not resuscitate order, a medical order for scope of treatment or an advance directive which outline my desire for a level of treatment other than full treatment will be temporarily suspended during the procedure unless otherwise requested by me.and documented by my physician in the medical record.

ANESTHESIA

| understand that this procedure may require anesthesia. An anesthesiology provider has or will explain the potential benefits, isks, side effects, and potential anesthesia alternatives. If your provider recommends and will manage local anesthesia or moderate sedation as noted below, this discussion will occur with your provider. Complications from any type of anesthetic are infrequent, but do occur. Complications are more frequent for patients in poor health than for patients in good health. Complications associated with all types of anesthesia include temporary or permanent damage to teeth, lips, nose, throat, voice box, windpipe, eyes, nerves, or blood vessels. Drug reaction may occur and may be minor or serious even requiring resuscitation. Breathing problems may develop. Headache, back pain, some degree of awareness, infection, nausea and/or vomiting may occur. There are some very serious complications, which are uncommon, butcan occur. These include paralysis, brain damage, or death. There are other potential complications depending on your situation, however, it is not possible to advise you of every imaginable complication. Serious complications are unlikely. The benefits of surgery and anesthesia, except in extreme circumstances, outweigh the potential risks. | agree that the potential benefit of the procedure(s) outweighs the potential risks of anesthesia.

i

| Monitored Anesthesia Care | Possible sedation, monitoring and care provided by the anes logist, a CRNA, o|

i [an AA, _

| Local Anesthesia _| Care managed by the Provider and monitored by a registered nurse. _ | Moderate Sedation _| Care managed by the Provider and monitored by a registered nurse.

There is the possibility that any of the services provided above may convert to a general anesthetic. These same complications may occur with local anesthesia. Additionally, anesthesiologists and providers may utilize monitoring devices such as arterial and Swan- Ganz lines or diagnostic tests such as Transesophageal Echocardiography [TEE] to assist in surgical procedures. More Information

Third Trimester of Pregnancy

The third trimester is from week 28 through week 40 (months 7 through 9). The third trimester is a time when the unborn baby (fetus) is growing rapidly. At the end of the ninth month, the fetus is about 20 inches in length and weighs 6—10 pounds.

Body changes during your third trimester

Your body will continue to go through many changes during pregnancy. The changes vary from woman to woman. During the third trimester:

Your weight will continue to increase. You can expect to gain 25—35 pounds (11—16 kg) by the end of the pregnancy.

You may begin to get stretch marks on your hips, abdomen, and breasts.

You may urinate more often because the fetus is moving lower into your pelvis and pressing on your bladder. You may develop or continue to have heartburn. This is caused by increased hormones that slow down muscles in the digestive tract.

You may develop or continue to have constipation because increased hormones slow digestion and cause the muscles that push waste through your intestines to relax.

You may develop hemorrhoids. These are swollen veins (varicose veins) in the rectum that can itch or be painful. You may develop swollen, bulging veins (varicose veins) in your legs.

You may have increased body aches in the pelvis, back, or thighs. This is due to weight gain and increased hormones that are relaxing your joints.

You may have changes in your hair. These can include thickening of your hair, rapid growth, and changes in texture. Some women also have hair loss during or after pregnancy, or hair that feels dry or thin. Your hair will most likely return to normal after your baby is born.

Your breasts will continue to grow and they will continue to become tender. A yellow fluid (colostrum) may leak from your breasts. This is the first milk you are producing for your baby.

Your belly button may stick out.

You may notice more swelling in your hands, face, or ankles.

You may have increased tingling or numbness in your hands, arms, and legs. The skin on your belly may also feel numb.

You may feel short of breath because of your expanding uterus.

You may have more problems sleeping. This can be caused by the size of your belly, increased need to urinate, and an increase in your body's metabolism.

You may notice the fetus "dropping," or moving lower in your abdomen (lightening).

You may have increased vaginal discharge.

You may notice your joints feel loose and you may have pain around your pelvic bone.

What to expect at prenatal visits

You will have prenatal exams every 2 weeks until week 36. Then you will have weekly prenatal exams. During a routine prenatal visit:

= a

You will be weighed to make sure you and the baby are growing normally. Your blood pressure will be taken.

Your abdomen will be measured to track your baby's growth.

The fetal heartbeat will be listened to.

Any test results from the previous visit will be discussed.

You may have a cervical check near your due date to see if your cervix has softened or thinned (effaced).

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 526




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1313], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1314] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued) More Information (continued)

e You will be tested for Group B streptococcus. This happens between 35 and 37 weeks. Your health care provider may ask you:

What your birth plan is.

How you are feeling.

If you are feeling the baby move.

If you have had any abnormal symptoms, such as leaking fluid, bleeding, severe headaches, or abdominal cramping.

e If you are using any tobacco products, including cigarettes, chewing tobacco, and electronic cigarettes.

e Ifyou have any questions.

Other tests or screenings that may be performed during your third trimester include:

e Blood tests that check for low iron levels (anemia).

e Fetal testing to check the health, activity level, and growth of the fetus. Testing is done if you have certain medical conditions or if there are problems during the pregnancy.

e Nonstress test (NST). This test checks the health of your baby to make sure there are no signs of problems, such as the baby not getting enough oxygen. During this test, a belt is placed around your belly. The baby is made to move, and its heart rate is monitored during movement.

What is false labor?

False labor is a condition in which you feel small, irregular tightenings of the muscles in the womb (contractions) that usually go away with rest, changing position, or drinking water. These are called Braxton Hicks contractions. Contractions may last for hours, days, or even weeks before true labor sets in. If contractions come at regular intervals, become more frequent, increase in intensity, or become painful, you should see your health care provider.

What are the signs of labor?

Abdominal cramps.

Regular contractions that start at 10 minutes apart and become stronger and more frequent with time. Contractions that start on the top of the uterus and spread down to the lower abdomen and back.

Increased pelvic pressure and dull back pain.

A watery or bloody mucus discharge that comes from the vagina.

Leaking of amniotic fluid. This is also known as your "water breaking." It could be a slow trickle or a gush. Let your health care provider know if it has a color or strange odor.

If you have any of these signs, call your health care provider right away, even if it is before your due date. Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Follow your health care provider's instructions regarding medicine use. Specific medicines may be either safe or unsafe to take during pregnancy.

e Take a prenatal vitamin that contains at least 600 micrograms (mcg) of folic acid.

e If you develop constipation, try taking a stool softener if your health care provider approves.

Eating and drinking

e Eat a balanced diet that includes fresh fruits and vegetables, whole grains, good sources of protein such as meat, eggs, or tofu, and low-fat dairy. Your health Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1315], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021 [DOB-1316] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued)

More Information (continued) care provider will help you determine the amount of weight gain that is right for you. e Avoid raw meat and uncooked cheese. These carry germs that can cause birth defects in the baby. e If you have low calcium intake from food, talk to your health care provider about whether you should take a daily calctum supplement. e Eat four or five small meals rather than three large meals a day. e Limit foods that are high in fat and processed sugars, such as fried and sweet foods. e To prevent constipation: o Drink enough fluid to keep your urine clear or pale yellow. © Eat foods that are high in fiber, such as fresh fruits and vegetables, whole grains, and beans.

Activity

e Exercise only as directed by your health care provider. Most women can continue their usual exercise routine during pregnancy. Try to exercise for 30 minutes at least 5 days a week. Stop exercising if you experience uterine contractions.

Avoid heavy lifting.

Do not exercise in extreme heat or humidity, or at high altitudes.

Wear low-heel, comfortable shoes.

Practice good posture.

You may continue to have sex unless your health care provider tells you otherwise.

Relieving pain and discomfort

e Take frequent breaks and rest with your legs elevated if you have leg cramps or low back pain. e Take warm sitz baths to soothe any pain or discomfort caused by hemorrhoids. Use hemorrhoid cream if your health care provider approves. e Wear a good support bra to prevent discomfort from breast tenderness. e If you develop varicose veins: o Wear support pantyhose or compression stockings as told by your healthcare provider. © Elevate your feet for 15 minutes, 3-4 times a day.

Prenatal care

e Write down your questions. Take them to your prenatal visits. ¢ Keep all your prenatal visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Safety

e Wear your seat belt at all times when driving. e Make a list of emergency phone numbers, including numbers for family, friends, the hospital, and police and fire departments.

General instructions

e Avoid cat litter boxes and soil used by cats. These carry germs that can cause birth defects in the baby. If you have a cat, ask someone to clean the litter box for you.

e Do not travel far distances unless it is absolutely necessary and only with the approval of your health care provider.

e Do not use hot tubs, steam rooms, or saunas.

e Do not drink alcohol.

e Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. If you need help quitting, ask your health care provider.

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 528




BAPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1317], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1318] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued)

More Information (continued)

Do not use any medicinal herbs or unprescribed drugs. These chemicals affect the formation and growth of the baby. Do not douche or use tampons or scented sanitary pads. Do not cross your legs for long periods of time. To prepare for the arrival of your baby: o Take prenatal classes to understand, practice, and ask questions about labor and delivery. Make a trial run to the hospital. Visit the hospital and tour the maternity area. Arrange for maternity or paternity leave through employers. Arrange for family and friends to take care of pets while you are in the hospital. Purchase a rear-facing car seat and make sure you know how to install it in your car. Pack your hospital bag. Prepare the baby’s nursery. Make sure to remove all pillows and stuffed animals from the baby's crib to prevent suffocation. Visit your dentist if you have not gone during your pregnancy. Use a soft toothbrush to brush your teeth and be gentle when you floss.

oO0O0000 0

Contact a health care provider if:

You are unsure if you are in labor or if your water has broken.

You become dizzy.

You have mild pelvic cramps, pelvic pressure, or nagging pain in your abdominal area. You have lower back pain.

You have persistent nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.

You have an unusual or bad smelling vaginal discharge.

You have pain when you urinate.

Get help right away if:

Your water breaks before 37 weeks.

You have regular contractions less than 5 minutes apart before 37 weeks. You have a fever.

You are leaking fluid from your vagina.

You have spotting or bleeding from your vagina.

You have severe abdominal pain or cramping.

You have rapid weight loss or weight gain.

You have shortness of breath with chest pain.

You notice sudden or extreme swelling of your face, hands, ankles, feet, or legs. Your baby makes fewer than 10 movements in 2 hours.

You have severe headaches that do not go away when you take medicine. You have vision changes.

Summary

The third trimester is from week 28 through week 40, months 7 through 9. The third trimester is a time when the unborn baby (fetus) is growing rapidly.

During the third trimester, your discomfort may increase as you and your baby continue to gain weight. You may have abdominal, leg, and back pain, sleeping problems, and an increased need to urinate.

During the third trimester your breasts will keep growing and they will continue to become tender. A yellow fluid (colostrum) may leak from your breasts. This is the first milk you are producing for your baby.

[DOB-1320] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued)

More Information (continued) e False labor is a condition in which you feel small, irregular tightenings of the muscles in the womb (contractions) that eventually go away. These are called Braxton Hicks contractions. Contractions may last for hours, days, or even weeks before true labor sets in. e Signs of labor can include: abdominal cramps; regular contractions that start at 10 minutes apart and become stronger and more frequent with time; watery or bloody mucus discharge that comes from the vagina; increased pelvic pressure and dull back pain; and leaking of amniotic fluid.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1323], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1324] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued) More Information (continued)

Nonstress Test

A nonstress test is a procedure that is done during pregnancy in order to check the baby's heartbeat. The procedure can help show if the baby (fetus) is healthy. It is eae

commonly done when: ATP

The baby is past his or her due date. fluid The pregnancy is high risk.

The baby is moving less than normal.

The mother has lost a pregnancy in the past.

The health care provider suspects a problem with the baby's growth. There is too much or too little amniotic fluid.

Fetus”

The procedure is often done in the third trimester of pregnancy to find out if an early delivery is needed and whether such a delivery is safe.

During a nonstress test, the baby's heartbeat is monitored when the baby is resting and when the baby is moving. If the baby is healthy, the heart rate will increase when he or she moves or kicks and will return to normal when he or she rests.

Tell a health care provider about:

e Any allergies you have. e Any medical conditions you have. e All medicines you are taking, including vitamins, herbs, eye drops, creams, and over-the-counter medicines.

What are the risks? There are no risks to you or your baby from a nonstress test. This procedure should not be painful or uncomfortable. What happens before the procedure?

e Eat a meal right before the test or as directed by your health care provider. Food may help encourage the baby to move. e Use the restroom right before the test.

What happens during the procedure?

e Two monitors will be placed on your abdomen. One will record the baby's heart rate and the other will record the contractions of your uterus.

e You may be asked to lie down on your side or to sit upright.

e You may be given a button to press when you feel your baby move.

e Your health care provider will listen to your baby's heartbeat and recorded it. He or she may also watch your baby's heartbeat on a screen.

e Ifthe baby seems to be sleeping, you may be asked to drink some juice or soda, eat a snack, or change positions.

The procedure may vary among health care providers and hospitals. What happens after the procedure?

e Your health care provider will discuss the test results with you and make recommendations for the future. Depending on the results, your health care provider may order additional tests or another course of action.

[DOB-1326] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued)

More Information (continued) e If your health care provider gave you any diet or activity instructions, make sure to follow them. ¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your health care provider. This is important.

Summary

e A nonstress test is a procedure that is done during pregnancy in order to check the baby's heartbeat. The procedure can help show if the baby is healthy.

e The procedure is often done in the third trimester of pregnancy to find out if an early delivery is needed and whether such a delivery is safe.

e During a nonstress test, the baby's heartbeat is monitored when the baby is resting and when the baby is moving. If the baby is healthy, the heart rate will increase when he or she moves or kicks and will return to normal when he or she rests.

e Your health care provider will discuss the test results with you and make recommendations for the future.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1329], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1330] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued) More Information (continued)

Signs and Symptoms of Labor

Labor is your body's natural process of moving your baby, placenta, and umbilical cord out of your uterus. The process of labor usually starts when your baby is full-term, between 37 and 40 weeks of pregnancy.

How will I know when I am close to going into labor?

As your body prepares for labor and the birth of your baby, you may notice the following symptoms in the weeks and days before true labor starts:

e Having a strong desire to get your home ready to receive your new baby. This is called nesting. Nesting may be a sign that labor is approaching, and it may occur several weeks before birth. Nesting may involve cleaning and organizing your home.

e Passing a small amount of thick, bloody mucus out of your vagina (normal bloody show or losing your mucus plug). This may happen more than a week before labor begins, or it might occur right before labor begins as the opening of the cervix starts to widen (dilate). For some women, the entire mucus plug passes at once. For others, smaller portions of the mucus plug may gradually pass over several days.

e Your baby moving (dropping) lower in your pelvis to get into position for birth (dightening). When this happens, you may feel more pressure on your bladder and pelvic bone and less pressure on your ribs. This may make it easier to breathe. It may also cause you to need to urinate more often and have problems with bowel movements.

e Having "practice contractions" (Braxton Hicks contractions) that occur at irregular (unevenly spaced) intervals that are more than 10 minutes apart. This is also called false labor. False labor contractions are common after exercise or sexual activity, and they will stop if you change position, rest, or drink fluids. These contractions are usually mild and do not get stronger over time. They may feel like:

o A backache or back pain. o Mild cramps, similar to menstrual cramps. o Tightening or pressure in your abdomen.

Other early symptoms that labor may be starting soon include:

Nausea or loss of appetite.

Diarrhea.

Having a sudden burst of energy, or feeling very tired. Mood changes.

Having trouble sleeping.

How will I know when labor has begun?

Signs that true labor has begun may include:

e Having contractions that come at regular (evenly spaced) intervals and increase in intensity. This may feel like more intense tightening or pressure in your abdomen that moves to your back. © Contractions may also feel like rhythmic pain in your upper thighs or back that comes and goes at regular intervals. © For first-time mothers, this change in intensity of contractions often occurs at a more gradual pace. o Women who have given birth before may notice a more rapid progression of contraction changes. e Having a feeling of pressure in the vaginal area. e Your water breaking (rupture of membranes). This is when the sac of fluid that surrounds your baby breaks. When this happens, you will notice fluid leaking from your vagina. This may be clear or blood-tinged. Labor

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 533




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1331], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/17/2021, D/C: 1/18/2021

[DOB-1332] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued)

More Information (continued) | usually starts within 24 hours of your water breaking, but it may take longer to begin. o Some women notice this as a gush of fluid. © Others notice that their underwear repeatedly becomes damp.

Follow these instructions at home:

e When labor starts, or if your water breaks, call your health care provider or nurse care line. Based on your situation, they will determine when you should go in for an exam. e When you are in early labor, you may be able to rest and manage symptoms at . home. Some strategies to try at home include: \ ——— Breathing and relaxation techniques. eal Taking a warm bath or shower. Listening to music. Using a heating pad on the lower back for pain. If you are directed to use heat: " Place a towel between your skin and the heat source. " Leave the heat on for 20-30 minutes. " Remove the heat if your skin turns bright red. This is especially important if you are unable to feel pain, heat, or cold. You may have a greater risk of getting burned.

oO O00 0

Get help right away if:

You have painful, regular contractions that are 5 minutes apart or less. Labor starts before you are 37 weeks along in your pregnancy. You have a fever. You have a headache that does not go away. You have bright red blood coming from your vagina. You do not feel your baby moving. You have a sudden onset of: o Severe headache with vision problems. o Nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. © Chest pain or shortness of breath.

These symptoms may be an emergency. If your health care provider recommends that you go to the hospital or birth center where you plan to deliver, do not drive yourself. Have someone else drive you, or call emergency services (911 in the U.S.)

Summary

e Labor is your body's natural process of moving your baby, placenta, and umbilical cord out of your uterus.

e The process of labor usually starts when your baby is full-term, between 37 and 40 weeks of pregnancy.

e When labor starts, or if your water breaks, call your health care provider or nurse care line. Based on your situation, they will determine when you should go in for an exam.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

[DOB-1336] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE LABOR DELIVERY (continued) More Information (continued)

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call 911 e Call a suicide hotline to speak to a counselor. 1-800-273-TALK or 1-800-SUICIDE

She was a restrained passenger in the backseat whose car was struck by a car from the rear. Damage to car is minimal. She had no LOC and states her baby has been active. She denies abdominal cramping or vaginal bleeding. She is approximately 37 weeks pregnant and is a patient of Dr. Lauren Lewis and plans to deliver here.

Author: Purvis, Gregory, MD Service: — Author Type: Physician Filed: 01/03/21 2111 Date of Service: 01/03/21 2035 Creation Time: 01/03/21 2035 Status: Signed Editor: Purvis, Gregory, MD (Physician)

Pt presents to the ED complaining of rear-ended MVA this evening. The patient was a restrained passenger in a car that was sitting at a stoplight and was rear-ended. She states she was wearing her seatbelt but the airbags did not deploy. She is complaining of some lower back pain but denies head injury or loss of consciousness and has been ambulatory since that time. The patient is approximately 37 weeks pregnant but denies abdominal pain or vaginal bleeding since the incident. She states she still feels the baby moving well.

On exam, pt is A&Ox3. NAD Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 546

Normal neuro exam

| agree with midlevel plan to check ultrasound here and send to L&D for fetal monitoring. The patient has a lumbar strain from her rear end MVA but does not need further imaging or work-up in the emergency room but will discuss with her OB about monitoring on L&D.

Chief Complaint: Back Pain and Motor Vehicle Crash (OBED around 1900. Backseat and restrained. Rear ended. Denies contractions, vaginal bleeding, and loss of fluid. +FM)

Subjective

Anella Noble is a 20 y.o. female G1P0 at 37w2d with Estimated Date of Delivery: 1/22/21 who presents with history of

MVA @ approximately 8 PM on 1/3/2021.

She sees Lewis, Lauren S, MD for her prenatal care. Her pregnancy has been uncomplicated to this point. She states she was a restrained passenger and that air bags did not deploy. She did not strike her abdomen. She denies seat belt marks and denies bruising. She reports her injuries as feeling some lower back pain following the accident. She was transported by EMS to Baptist East Main ER where she was seen and evaluated for her low back pain and cleared prior to coming to OB ED. She denies any contractions or abdominal pain she denies any vaginal bleeding or loss of fluid and she is feeling normal fetal movement.

The patient denies fever, cough, cold or flu symptoms, shortness of breath, or changes in taste or smell.

Objective There are no active problems to display for this patient.

Assessment/Plan

ASSESSMENT/PLAN:

Anella Noble is a 20 y.o. female G1P0 at 37w2d who presented following an MVA @ approximately 20:00 on

1/3/2021. She states she was a restrained passenger on the passengers side and that air bags did not deploy. She did not strike her abdomen. She denies seat belt marks and denies bruising. She reports her injuries as: new onset of low back pain. She states that the car had minimal damage from the accident, which describes as being rear ended.. She was watched for 4 hours (or more) from the time of the accident and no evidence of significant obstetrical trauma was noted. She was noted to have a Reactive NST and was watched for approximately 3.5 hour(s). In view of no significant OB trauma she will be discharged to home.

Final Impression: e Pregnancy at 37w2d

e Reactive NST, CAT 1 tracing, Reassuring fetal status e S/P MVA with no significant obstetrical trauma e Negative KB and no evidence for abruption e Maternal vital signs were reviewed and were unremarkable Vitals: 01/03/21 2304 01/03/21 2306 01/03/21 2308 01/03/21 2310 BP: BP Location: Patient Position: Pulse: 70 84 79 70 Resp: Temp: TempSrc: SpO2: 99% 97% 98% 98% Weight: Height:

e UAno evidence of UTI e OPOSITIVE and Rhogam was not indicated e She will be discharged to home

Initial Physical Exam General

ra GENERAL APPEARANCE - Alert, Cooperative, Oriented x 3, Well-developed, Well nourished, Not in acute distress. Skin Head and Neck Thyroid gland Characteristics - normal size and consistency. Nec Characteristics - no lymphadenopathy.

Chest and Lung Exam

Chest and lung exam reveals - quiet, even and easy respiratory effort with no use of accessory muscles and on auscultation, normal breath sounds, no adventitious sounds and normal vocal resonance.

Breast

Nipples ; ; . Characteristics - Bilateral - No discharge and Non-tender, Not Dimpled, No Erythema, Not Inverted, No Retraction.

Breast - Bilateral - Non-tender, breast exam performed in the sitting and supine position and Symmetric, No Dimpling, No Erythema, no breast masses, no Thickening of skin.

Cardiovascular ;

Cardiovascular examination reveals - normal heart sounds, regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs.

Abdomen

Normal Exam - Soft, Non-tender, No guarding, No Hepatosplenomegaly, No rebound and No mass.

Inspection

Inspection of the abdomen reveals - No Hernias.

Female Genitourinary Pelvic Exam

External genitalia - Vulva - Normal, No Lesion. Clitoris - Normal. Introitus - Normal and No Discharge. Urethra - Normal. Speculum & Bimanual

Cervix - Characteristics - Normal. Discharge - None. Uterus - Characteristics - Smooth, Non Tender. Fundal Height - Note: 12 week uterine size. Vaginal Mucosa - Normal appearance. Adnexa - Characteristics - Bilateral - Non Tender, Ovary not enlarged. Masses - No Adnexal Masses. Bladder - Normal.

Rectal

Anorectal Exam

External - normal external exam.

Neurologic

Quick NORMAL Neurological Exam - Gross motor function intact, No focal motor deficits and Normal affect, speech and thought processes.

Lymphatic

General Lymphatics

BF oo ApTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1501], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/3/2021, D/C: 1/4/2021 [DOB-1502] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

Care Plan (continued)

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Intervention: Identify and Manage Fall Risk Frequency: Per CPG Description: Perform standard risk assessment with a validated tool or comprehensive approach

appropriate to the patient on admission; reassess fall risk frequently, with change in status or transfer to another level of care.

Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team.

Determine need for increased observation, equipment and environmental modification, such as low bed and signage, as well as supportive, nonskid footwear.

Adjust safety measures to individual developmental age, stage and identified risk factors. Reinforce the importance of safety and physical activity with patient and family.

Perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment, personal needs, including assistance with toileting.

Intervention: Prevent Skin Injury

Frequency: Per CPG Description: Assess skin risk on admission and at regular intervals throughout hospital stay. Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry. Maintain adequate skin hydration. Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based on patient-specific risk factors. Match turning and repositioning schedule to clinical condition. Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition if unable to complete independently. Float heels off bed. Avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon. Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices. Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.

Intervention: Prevent and Manage VTE (venous thromboembolism) Risk

Frequency: Per CPG Description: Assess for VTE risk. Encourage/assist with early ambulation. Initiate and maintain compression or other therapy, as indicated based on identified risk in accordance with organizational protocol/provider order. Encourage both active and passive leg exercises while in bed, if unable to ambulate.

Intervention: Prevent Infection

Frequency: Per CPG

Description: Maintain skin and mucous membrane integrity; promote hand, oral and pulmonary hygiene. Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance. Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g., wound, lines, devices). Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated.

Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Intervention: Monitor Pain and Promote Comfort Frequency: Per CPG Description: Assess pain level, treatment efficacy and patient response at regular intervals using a

consistent pain scale.

Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain.

Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety measures.

Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care

Frequency: Per CPG Description: Use a family-focused approach to care. Develop trust and rapport by proactively providing information, encouraging questions, addressing concerns and offering reassurance. Acknowledge emotional response to hospitalization. Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 588




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1503], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/3/2021, D/C: 1/4/2021 [DOB-1504] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

Care Plan (continued)

Recognize and utilize personal coping strategies. Honor spiritual and cultural preferences.

Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care

Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary Intervention: Mutually Develop Transition Plan Frequency: Per CPG Description: Identify available resources for support (e.g., family, friends, community).

Identify and address barriers to ongoing treatment and home management (e.g., environmental, financial).

Provide opportunities to practice self-management skills.

Assess and monitor emotional readiness for transition.

Establish/reconnect linkage with outpatient providers or community-based services.

Patient Education (continued)

Point: Fall Prevention (Resolved)

Description:

The healthcare setting is an unfamiliar environment. Instruct the patient and family to call for help with activity to prevent a fall, even if feeling able to do things independently. Keep the room and pathway to bathroom clutter-free. Caution patient and family not to disconnect or silence any alarms.

Patient Friendly Description: We don't want you to fall.

Call for help, even if you feel like you can do things on your own. Keep the room and the path to bathroom clear.

Never disconnect or silence any alarms. Learning Progress Summary

Patient Acceptance, Explanation, Teach Back, Verbalizes Understanding by JS at 1/3/2021 2330

Point: Call Light Use (Resolved)

Description: Review call light use and features with the patient and family. Instruct the patient to always keep it in reach. Note: Evaluate physical and cognitive ability to determine appropriate call device.

Patient Friendly Description: Request help when needed by:

using the call light talking to the nurse or another healthcare team member

Someone will be checking on you often. Learning Progress Summary

Assessment Time: [DOB-1563] 20:12:05

Dispatched code 3 on a MVA. Arrived on scene along with LMPD. 2 vehicles were involved, a white suv and a white sedan. Both vehicles did not appear to have any damage to them. The white sedan had 3 occupants that were female. The patient was a 20 year old female restrained passenger in the back seat. Patient was 37 weeks pregnant with her first child. Patient states that they were stopped at a stop sign when their vehicle got struck in the back by a white suv. The occupants in the suv jumped out a fled the scene. No airbag deployment in either vehicle. The patient stated that the car just shook. The patient denies head or neck pain. The patient states that she has been having lower back pain but just wants to make sure that nothing has happen to the baby because of the accident. Patient skin is pink, warm, and dry. Pupils are PERRL. Lung sounds are clear equal and bilateral. Patient has proper PMS function in all extremities. Patient abdomen is soft, non tender. Patient denies LOC, SOA, headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, chest pain or abdomen pain. Patient vitals are within normal limits and are as noted. Patient requested transport to Baptist East Hospital for further evaluation. Transported code 1 to Baptist East Hospital. Continued to monitor patients vitals and condition without remarkable changes. Arrived at Baptist East Hospital without any incidents. Transferred care to ED staff along with a verbal patient care report for further evaluation. All times are approximate.

37 weeks pregnant

lower back pain

Baptist

minor to no damage to car. but ennough no air bag deployment

sitting at stop sign

2. PHYSICIANS AND OTHER HEALTH CARE PROVIDERS WHO ARE NOT EMPLOYEES OF THE HOSPITAL:

Physicians and certified registered nurse anesthetists rendering medical treatment to patients in the hospital are not employed by the Hospital. In most situations, advanced practitioners, including but not limited to, nurse practitioners, physician assistants, surgical assistants and psychologists, providing treatment to patients in the Hospital are not employed by the Hospital. You may receive a separate bill for the services of these providers, or the Hospital bill you receive may have separate charges for services of such providers. Charges for such services are established by the providers.

More Information

Motor Vehicle Collision Injury, Adult

After a car accident (motor vehicle collision), it is common to have injuries to your head, face, arms, and body. These injuries may include:

Cuts.

Burns.

Bruises.

Sore muscles or a stretch or tear in a muscle (strain).

Headaches.

You may feel stiff and sore for the first several hours. You may feel worse after waking up the first morning after the accident. These injuries often feel worse for the first 24-48 hours. After that, you will usually begin to get better with each day. How quickly you get better often depends on:

How bad the accident was.

How many injuries you have. Where your injuries are.

What types of injuries you have. If you were wearing a seat belt. If your airbag was used.

A head injury may result in a concussion. This is a type of brain injury that can have serious effects. If you have a concussion, you should rest as told by your doctor. You must be very careful to avoid having a second concussion.

Follow these instructions at home: Medicines

e Take over-the-counter and prescription medicines only as told by your doctor. e If you were prescribed antibiotic medicine, take or apply it as told by your doctor. Do not stop using the antibiotic even if your condition gets better.

If you have a wound or a burn:

e Clean your wound or burn as told by your doctor. Wash it with mild soap and water. © Rinse it with water to get all the soap off. o Pat it dry with a clean towel. Do not rub it. © If you were told to put an ointment or cream on the wound, do so as told by your doctor. e Follow instructions from your doctor about how to take care of your wound or burn. Make sure you: o Know when and how to change or remove your bandage (dressing). o Always wash your hands with soap and water before and after you change your bandage. If you cannot use soap and water, use hand sanitizer. o Leave stitches (sutures), skin glue, or skin tape (adhesive) strips in place, if you have these. They may need to stay in place for 2 weeks or longer. If tape strips get loose and curl up, you may trim the loose edges. Do not remove tape strips completely unless your doctor says it is okay.

(e)

_ fF

Normal Infected wound wound

SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1591], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/3/2021, D/C: 1/4/2021

[DOB-1592] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued) ||

e Do not:

© Scratch or pick at the wound or burn. o Break any blisters you may have. © Peel any skin.

e Avoid getting sun on your wound or burn.

e Raise (e/evate) the wound or burn above the level of your heart while you are sitting or lying down. If you have a wound or burn on your face, you may want to sleep with your head raised. You may do this by putting an extra pillow under your head.

e Check your wound or burn every day for signs of infection. Check for:

© More redness, swelling, or pain. More fluid or blood. Warmth. Pus or a bad smell.

Oo 0 O

Activity

e Rest. Rest helps your body to heal. Make sure you: © Get plenty of sleep at night. Avoid staying up late. © Go to bed at the same time on weekends and weekdays. e Ask your doctor if you have any limits to what you can lift. e Ask your doctor when you can drive, ride a bicycle, or use heavy machinery. Do not do these activities if you are dizzy. e Ifyou are told to wear a brace on an injured arm, leg, or other part of your body, follow instructions from your doctor about activities. Your doctor may give you instructions about driving, bathing, exercising, or working.

General instructions

e If told, put ice on the injured areas. © Put ice in a plastic bag. = | ee | eek © Place a towel between your skin and the bag. a

© Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2—3 times a day. e Drink enough fluid to keep your pee (urine) pale yellow. e Do not drink alcohol. e Eat healthy foods. ¢ Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor. This is important.

Contact a doctor if:

Your symptoms get worse.

You have neck pain that gets worse or has not improved after 1 week.

You have signs of infection in a wound or burn.

You have a fever.

You have any of the following symptoms for more than 2 weeks after your car accident: o Lasting (chronic) headaches. © Dizziness or balance problems. o Feeling sick to your stomach (nauseous).

Printed on 4/20/21 8:15 AM Page 607




SF oo aPTIST HEALTH’ Baptist Health Louisville Noble, Anella or 4000 Kresge Way MRN: [PHONE-1593], DOB: 11/7/2000, Sex: F Louisville KY 40207-4605 Adm: 1/3/2021, D/C: 1/4/2021

[DOB-1594] - ED to Hosp-Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT (continued)

More Information (continued) © Problems with how you see (vision).

More sensitivity to noise or light.

Depression or mood swings.

Feeling worried or nervous (anxiety).

Getting upset or bothered easily.

Memory problems.

Trouble concentrating or paying attention.

Sleep problems.

Feeling tired all the time.

0o0O00006dCO0OUlLUO

Get help right away if:

e You have: © Loss of feeling (numbness), tingling, or weakness in your arms or legs. Very bad neck pain, especially tenderness in the middle of the back of your neck. A change in your ability to control your pee or poop (stoo/). More pain in any area of your body. Swelling in any area of your body, especially your legs. Shortness of breath or light-headedness. Chest pain. Blood in your pee, poop, or vomit. Very bad pain in your belly (abdomen) or your back. Very bad headaches or headaches that are getting worse. o Sudden vision loss or double vision. e Your eye suddenly turns red. e The black center of your eye (pupil) is an odd shape or size.

oO0O000606UC0UdlUCOUlUCO

Summary

After a car accident (motor vehicle collision), it is common to have injuries to your head, face, arms, and body. Follow instructions from your doctor about how to take care of a wound or burn.

If told, put ice on your injured areas.

Contact a doctor if your symptoms get worse.

Keep all follow-up visits as told by your doctor.

This information is not intended to replace advice given to you by your health care provider. Make sure you discuss any questions you have with your health care provider.

Document Revised: [DOB-1595] Document Reviewed: [DOB-1596] Elsevier Patient Education © 2020 Elsevier Inc.

Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding

Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye

Sudden severe headache with no known cause

Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination

It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get

emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a stroke.

For more information: American Stroke Association 1-888-4-STROKE www.strokeassociation.org

IF YOU SMOKE OR USE TOBACCO PLEASE READ THE FOLLOWING:

Why is smoking bad for me?

Smoking increases the risk of heart disease, lung disease, vascular disease, stroke, and cancer. If you smoke, STOP!

For more information:

Quit Now Kentucky 1-800-QUIT-NOW https://kentucky.quitlogix.org/en-US/

If you feel like life is too tough and are thinking of suicide or injuring yourself, get help right away! e Call 911 e Call a suicide hotline to speak to a counselor. 1-800-273-TALK or 1-800-SUICIDE

Narrative

Dispatched code 3 on a MVA. Arrived on scene along with LMPD. 2 vehicles were involved, a white suv and a white sedan. Both vehicles did not appear to have any damage to them. The white sedan had 3 occupants that were female. The patient was a 20 year old female restrained passenger in the back seat. Patient was 37 weeks pregnant with her first child. Patient states that they were stopped at a stop sign when their vehicle got struck in the back by a white suv. The occupants in the suv jumped out a fled the scene. No airbag deployment in either vehicle. The patient stated that the car just shook. The patient denies head or neck pain. The patient states that she has been having lower back pain but just wants to make sure that nothing has happen to the baby because of the accident. Patient skin is pink, warm, and dry. Pupils are PERRL. Lung sounds are clear equal and bilateral. Patient has proper PMS function in all extremities. Patient abdomen is soft, non tender. Patient denies LOC, SOA, headache, dizziness, weakness, nausea, chest pain or abdomen pain. Patient vitals are within normal limits and are as noted. Patient requested transport to Baptist East Hospital for further evaluation. Transported code 1 to Baptist East Hospital. Continued to monitor patients vitals and condition without

remarkable changes. Arrived at Baptist East Hospital without any incidents. Transferred care to ED staff along with a verbal patient care report for further evaluation. All times are approximate.

37 weeks pregnant

lower back pain

Baptist

minor to no damage to car. but ennough no air bag deployment

sitting at stop sign

She denies abdominal cramping or vaginal bleeding. She is approximately 37 weeks pregnant and is a patient of Dr. Lauren Lewis and plans to deliver here. o-

Notes by Purvis, Gregory, MD at 1/3/2021 8235 PM cccccusennsearnsisneessesseNNONSES —cccaceceaeeeuacananesesesseereseeeeeeessssesssueunssssees ERAS RC ER RN SSMS eS ERNE TESS) NAN CSS ES RS EC 3 a: Physician Service: 01/03/21 2035 Cxeation Tinie: 01/09/21 2035 ca: Purvis, Gregory, MD (Physician)

Pt presents to the ED complaining of rear-ended MVA this evening. The patient was a restrained passenger in a car that was sitting at a stoplight and was rear-ended. She states she was wearing her seatbelt but the airbags did not deploy. She is complaining of some lower back pain but denies head injury or loss of consciousness and has been ambulatory since that time. The patient is approximately 37 weeks pregnant but denies abdominal pain or vaginal bleeding since the incident. She states she still feels the baby moving well.

On exam, pt is A&Ox3. NAD

PERRL, moist mucous membranes.

Normocephalic and atraumatic. No C-spine tenderness

Heart is RRR. Lungs are CTAB.

Abd is soft, non tender, gravid, non distended, bowel sounds positive.

No pedal edema. No calf tenderness.

Normal neuro exam

The patient has minimal L-spine tenderness with full range of motion without pain. She has no step-off and is neurovascular intact in her lower extremities.

| agree with midlevel plan to check ultrasound here and send to L&D for fetal monitoring. The patient has a lumbar strain from her rear end MVA but does not need further imaging or work-up in the emergency room but will discuss with her OB about monitoring on L&D.

PPE Pt does not present with symptoms for COVID19; however, | was wearing a mask and goggles throughout all patient interaction.

The APP and | have discussed this patient's history, physical exam, and treatment plan. | have reviewed the documentation and personally had a face to face interaction with the patient. | affirm the documentation and agree with the treatment and plan. The attached note describes my personal findings.

Purvis, Gregory, MD 01/03/21 2111

Patient Name: Anella Noble Date of onset: January 3, 2021 Patient's DOB: [DOB-2] Date of visit: March 19, 2021 Doctor's Name: Robert Black, DC Visit #: 16 of 16 Subjective

P - Pain/Tenderness

Ms. Noble presented to the office today with the following complaints:

Right neck pain, which she rated 3 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom radiates to the right shoulder.

Low back pain, which she rated 5 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate. .

Right shoulder pain, which she rated 5 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 100% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate.

Objective

A - Asymmetry/Misalignment Restrictions were noted at the following spinal levels: C5, C6, C7, L3, L4 and L5. Upper extremity restrictions were noted in the right shoulder.

R - Range of Motion Abnormality

A visual assessment of cervical range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of lumbar range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of shoulder range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: right abduction and right adduction.

T - Tissue/Tone Changes

Muscle spasms were noted in the following muscles: right trapezius, right levator scapulae, right rhomboid and bilateral erector spinae.

Myofascial trigger points were noted in the following muscles: right trapezius, right levator scapulae and bilateral erector spinae.

Muscle guarding was noted in the following muscles: bilateral lumbar paraspinal.

Assessment

Neck Pain :

- Pain intensity: Previous: 3/10 Current: 3/10 Pain intensity remained the same.

- Pain frequency: Previous: 100% Current: 100% Pain frequency remained the same.

- Acute (< 4 weeks since onset)

Low Back Pain: - Pain intensity: Previous: 5/10 Current: 5/10

Pain intensity remained the same.

- Pain frequency: Previous: 100% Current: 100% Pain frequency remained the same.

- Acute (< 4 weeks since onset)

Shoulder Pain:

- Pain intensity: Previous: 5/10 Current: 5/10 Pain intensity remained the same.

- Pain frequency: Previous: 100% Current: 100% Pain frequency remained the same.

- Acute (< 4 weeks since onset)

Diagnoses: * S16.1XXD Strain of muscle, fascia and tendon at neck level, Subsequent Encounter + M99.01 Segmental and somatic dysfunction of cervical region ¢ M54.13 Radiculopathy, cervicothoracic region + $39.012D Strain of muscle, fascia and tendon of low back, Subsequent Encounter *M99.03 Segmental and somatic dysfunction of lumbar region

* $46.911D Strain of unspecified muscle, fascia and tendon at shoulder and upper arm level, right arm, Subsequent Encounter

» M99.07 Segmental and somatic dysfunction of upper extremity (shoulder)

Plan

Spinal Adjustments: Chiropractic manipulative therapy (98940) was made to the following restricted spinal segments: C5, C6, C7, L3, L4, L5.

Upper Extremity Adjustments: Chiropractic manipulative therapy (98943) was made to the following restricted extremities: right shoulder.

Heat: Heat (97010) was applied to the Cervical, Lumbar for 15 minutes in order to increase bload flow to the area and relax the muscles.

Mechanical traction:

She was placed on an intersegmental traction table (97012) for 10 minutes in order to promote muscle relaxation and improve circulation and intersegmental movement.

Hydrotherapy: She was placed on a hydromassage table (97039) for 15 minutes in order to promote muscle relaxation and improve circulation and intersegmental movement.

Response to Treatment: Patient responded well to treatment and had no adverse reaction.

We will treat Ms. Noble as per the examination findings and continue the existing treatment plan. It is recommended that she return for treatment three times a week.

P - Pain/Tenderness

Ms. Noble presented to the office today with the following complaints:

Right neck pain, which she rated 3 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom radiates to the right shoulder.

Low back pain, which she rated 5 out of 10, ona scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate. .

Right shoulder pain, which she rated 5 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 100% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate.

Objective

R - Range of Motion Abnormality

A visual assessment of cervical range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of lumbar range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of shoulder range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: right abduction and right adduction.

T - Tissue/Tone Changes

- Physiologic Effects of Radiant Heat

Patient Name: Anella Nobile Date of onset: January 3, 2021 Patient's DOB: [DOB-2] Date of visit: March 15, 2021 Doctor's Name: Robert Black, DC Visit #: 14 of 14 Subjective

P - Pain/Tenderness

Ms. Noble presented to the office today with the following complaints:

Right neck pain, which she rated 4 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom radiates to the right shoulder. Referring patient to MD. Ordering an cervical MRI: necessary due to consistent high levels of pain, patient's failure to respond to conservative treatment and possible ligament/disc injuries.

Low back pain, which she rated 7 out of 10, ona scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate. Ordering an lumbar MRI: necessary due to consistent high levels of pain, patient's failure to respond to conservative treatment and possible ligament/disc injuries.

Right shouider pain, which she rated 5 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 100% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate.

Objective

A - Asymmetry/Misalignment Restrictions were noted at the following spinal levels: C5, C6, C7, L3, L4 and L5. Upper extremity restrictions were noted in the right shoulder.

R - Range of Motion Abnormality

A visual assessment of cervical range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of Jumbar range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of shoulder range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: right abduction and right adduction.

T - Tissue/Tone Changes

Muscle spasms were noted in the following muscles: right trapezius, right levator scapulae, right rhomboid and bilateral erector spinae.

Myofascial trigger points were noted in the following muscles: right trapezius, right levator scapulae and bilateral erector spinae.

Muscle guarding was noted in the following muscles: bilateral lumbar paraspinal.

Assessment

Neck Pain :

- Pain intensity: Previous: 4/10 Current: 4/10 Pain intensity remained the same.

- Pain frequency: Previous: 100% Current: 100% Pain frequency remained the same.

- Acute (< 4 weeks since onset)

Physiologic Effects of Radiant Heat

Analgesic — This effect is the most frequent indication for the application of heat. Current thought

suggests this effect is related to the gate control theory of pain modulation.

guarding. [t also increases the elasticity of connective tissue which is an important consideration in

Ms. Noble presented to the office today with the following complaints:

Right neck pain, which she rated 4 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 400% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom radiates to the right shoulder, Baby delivered on [DOB-1710].

Low back pain, which she rated 7 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 100% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate.

Right shoulder pain, which she rated 5 out of 10, on a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst. This symptom occurs 100% of her awake time. Ms. Noble noted that this symptom does not radiate.

R - Range of Motion Abnormality

A visual assessment of cervical range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of lumbar range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the following planes of motion: flexion, extension, left lateral flexion, right lateral flexion, left rotation and right rotation. A visual assessment of shoulder range of motion revealed an apparent limitation and an increase in pain in the

following planes of motion: right abduction and right adduction.

T - Tissue/Tone Changes

Muscle spasms were noted in the following muscles: right trapezius, right levator scapulae, right rhomboid and bilateral erector spinae.

Myofascial trigger points were noted in the following muscles: right trapezius, right levator scapulae and bilateral erector spinae.

Muscle guarding was noted in the following muscles: bilateral lumbar paraspinal.

Patient Name: Anella Noble Date of onset: January 3, 2021 Patient's DOB: [DOB-2] Date of visit: March 8, 2021 Doctor's Name: Robert Black, DC Visit #: 12 of 12 Assessment

Pain intensity regressed by 67% from previous visit. - Pain frequency: Previous: 100% Current: 100% Pain frequency remained the same.

- Acute (< 4 weeks since onset)

Injury Causation

In my opinion, based on the history and my evaluation, the injuries suffered by Ms. Noble are a direct result of the

motor vehicle collision of [DOB-1720].

According to the article, "A practicable and systematic approach to medicolegal causation,” rules for assessing potential causal relationships have been formalized in the form of the “Hill criteria.” The Hill criteria is broken down to 3 basic elements for practical application in clinical assessments of causation.

A recent legal decision from the 10th Circuit US Court of Appeals endorsed the 3-step causal methodology as the generally accepted approach for injury causation.

4. Plausibility. This first step addresses whether it is biologically possible for the injury event to have caused the condition. Epidemiologic and other scientific literature can serve as a basis for a determination of plausibility, but clinical experience is also an adequate basis for the determination. It is appropriate to use prior clinical experience with an injury mechanism, gleaned from patient histories, as a foundation for assessing whether an injury plausibly resulted from a similar injury mechanism.

2. Temporality. This second step, which examines the clinical and other evidence of the timing between the onset of the symptoms of injury and the injury event, must be satisfied to assess the specific cause of the patient's injury. First, it must be established that the sequence of the injury and the event is appropriate; the symptoms cannot be identically present prior to the event. Further, the onset of the symptoms of injury cannot be implausibly latent, relative to the injury event.

3. Lack of a more probable alternative explanation. This final step is akin to a differential etiology in that the probability of alternative explanations is assessed and weighed relative to the injury risk of the traumatic event in question. Any evidence of an intervening injury event must be evaluated as the most obvious competing explanation. The estimation of the likelihood of the competing explanation can come from clinical experience, epidemiologic study, or a combination of sources.
